**Turin**[[a]](#cite_note-4) ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Torino*)[[b]](#cite_note-5) is a city and an important business and cultural centre in [northern](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy") [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"). It is the capital city of the [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont") region and of the [Metropolitan City of Turin](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Turin "Metropolitan City of Turin"). From 1861 to 1865, it was the first capital of the Kingdom of Italy. The city is mainly on the western bank of the [River Po](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)"), below its [Susa Valley](/wiki/Susa_Valley "Susa Valley"), and is surrounded by the western [Alpine arch](/wiki/Alpine_arch "Alpine arch") and [Superga](/wiki/Superga "Superga") hill. The population of the city proper is 853,196 as of 2025,[[4]](#cite_note-6) while the population of the urban area is estimated by [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat") to be 1.7 million inhabitants.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Turin metropolitan area](/wiki/Turin_metropolitan_area "Turin metropolitan area") is estimated by the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") to have a population of 2.2 million.[[5]](#cite_note-competitive1-7)

The city was historically a major European political centre. From 1563, it was the capital of the [Duchy of Savoy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Savoy "Duchy of Savoy"), then of the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)") ruled by the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"), and then the first capital of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") from 1861 to 1865.[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-9) Turin is sometimes called "the cradle of Italian liberty" for having been the political and intellectual centre of the *Risorgimento* that led to the [unification of Italy](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy"),[[8]](#cite_note-Britannica-10) as well as the birthplace of notable individuals who contributed to it, such as [Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour](/wiki/Camillo_Benso,_Count_of_Cavour "Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour").[[9]](#cite_note-11)

Although much of its political influence had been lost by [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it had become a centre of [anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascist "Anti-fascist") movements during the *[Ventennio fascista](/wiki/Ventennio_fascista "Ventennio fascista")*, including the [Italian resistance movement](/wiki/Italian_resistance_movement "Italian resistance movement"). Postwar Turin became a major European crossroads for industry, commerce and trade, and is part of the industrial triangle, along with [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") and [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"). It is ranked third in Italy, after Milan and [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), for economic strength.[[10]](#cite_note-12)

As of 2018, the city has been ranked by [GaWC](/wiki/GaWC "GaWC") as a Gamma-level [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city").[[11]](#cite_note-13) Turin is home to much of the [Italian automotive industry](/wiki/Italian_automotive_industry "Italian automotive industry"), and is the base for the headquarters of [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat"), [Lancia](/wiki/Lancia "Lancia"), and [Alfa Romeo](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo "Alfa Romeo").[[8]](#cite_note-Britannica-10)[[12]](#cite_note-14)

The city has a rich culture and history, and is known for its numerous [art galleries](/wiki/Art_galleries "Art galleries"), restaurants, churches, palaces, [opera houses](/wiki/Opera_house "Opera house"), [piazzas](/wiki/Piazza "Piazza"), parks, gardens, theatres, libraries, museums and other venues. Turin is well known for its [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque"), [Rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo"), [Neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism"), and [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau") architecture.

Many of Turin's [public squares](/wiki/Public_squares "Public squares"), castles, gardens, and elegant *[palazzi](/wiki/Palazzo "Palazzo")*, such as the [Palazzo Madama](/wiki/Palazzo_Madama,_Turin "Palazzo Madama, Turin"), were built between the 16th and 18th centuries. A part of the historical centre of Turin was inscribed in the [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List") under the name [Residences of the Royal House of Savoy](/wiki/Residences_of_the_Royal_House_of_Savoy "Residences of the Royal House of Savoy").

In addition, the city is home to museums, such as the [Museo Egizio](/wiki/Museo_Egizio "Museo Egizio"),[[13]](#cite_note-15) and the [Mole Antonelliana](/wiki/Mole_Antonelliana "Mole Antonelliana"), the city's architectural symbol, which in turn hosts the [National Museum of Cinema](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Cinema "National Museum of Cinema"). Turin's attractions make it one of the world's top 250 tourist destinations and the tenth-most visited city in Italy in 2008.

The city also hosts some of Italy's most important universities, colleges, academies, [lycea](/wiki/Lycea "Lycea"), and [gymnasia](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)"), such as the [University of Turin](/wiki/University_of_Turin "University of Turin"), founded in the 15th century, and the [Turin Polytechnic](/wiki/Turin_Polytechnic "Turin Polytechnic").[[14]](#cite_note-euromonitor.com-16) Turin is also famous worldwide for [icons](/wiki/Cultural_icon "Cultural icon") such as the [Shroud of Turin](/wiki/Shroud_of_Turin "Shroud of Turin"), the *[gianduiotto](/wiki/Gianduiotto "Gianduiotto")*, the automobile brand Fiat, and the association football club [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC"), which competes with its rival [Torino](/wiki/Torino_FC "Torino FC") in the [Derby della Mole](/wiki/Derby_della_Mole "Derby della Mole"), the city's [derby](/wiki/Football_derbies_in_Italy "Football derbies in Italy").[[15]](#cite_note-17) The city was one of the host cities of the [1934](/wiki/1934_FIFA_World_Cup "1934 FIFA World Cup")[[16]](#cite_note-18) and [1990](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup") FIFA World Cups,[[17]](#cite_note-19) and also hosted the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"), the [Eurovision Song Contest 2022](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_2022 "Eurovision Song Contest 2022") and the tennis [ATP Finals](/wiki/ATP_Finals "ATP Finals") from 2021 to 2026.[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)

### Ancient origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ancient origins")]

The [Taurini](/wiki/Taurini "Taurini") were an ancient [Celto-Ligurian](/wiki/Celto-Ligurian "Celto-Ligurian"),[[20]](#cite_note-22) [Alpine](/wiki/Alpine_race "Alpine race") people, who occupied the upper valley of the [River Po](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)"), in the centre of modern [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont"). In 218 BC, they were attacked by [Hannibal](/wiki/Hannibal "Hannibal") as he was allied with their long-standing enemies, the [Insubres](/wiki/Insubres "Insubres"). The Taurini chief town (*Taurasia*) was captured by Hannibal's forces after a three-day siege.[[21]](#cite_note-23) As a people they are rarely mentioned in history. It is believed that a [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") colony was established after 28 BC under the name of *Julia Augusta Taurinorum* (modern Turin). Both [Livy](/wiki/Livy "Livy")[[22]](#cite_note-24) and [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo")[[23]](#cite_note-25) mention the Taurini's country as including one of the passes of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"), which points to a wider use of the name in earlier times.

### Roman era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Roman era")]

In the 1st century BC, the Romans founded *Augusta Taurinorum*. Via Garibaldi traces the exact path of the Roman city's [decumanus](/wiki/Decumanus_Maximus "Decumanus Maximus") which began at the *Porta Decumani*, later incorporated into the *Castello* or [Palazzo Madama](/wiki/Palazzo_Madama,_Turin "Palazzo Madama, Turin"). The [Porta Palatina](/wiki/Porta_Palatina "Porta Palatina"), on the north side of the current city centre, is still preserved in a park near the cathedral. Remains of the Roman-period [theatre](/wiki/Roman_theatre_(structure) "Roman theatre (structure)") are preserved in the area of the *Manica Nuova*. Turin reached about 5,000 inhabitants at the time, all living inside the high city walls.

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

After the fall of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), the town, along with the rest of the Italian peninsula, was conquered by the [Heruli](/wiki/Heruli "Heruli") and the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths"), recaptured by the [Romans](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), but then conquered again by the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") whose territory then fell into the hands of the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") under [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") (773). The *[Contea di Torino](/wiki/March_of_Turin "March of Turin")* (countship) was founded in the 940s and was held by the [Arduinic](/wiki/Arduin_of_Italy "Arduin of Italy") dynasty until 1050. After the marriage of [Adelaide of Susa](/wiki/Adelaide_of_Susa "Adelaide of Susa") with [Humbert Biancamano](/wiki/Humbert_I,_Count_of_Savoy "Humbert I, Count of Savoy")'s son [Otto](/wiki/Otto,_Count_of_Savoy "Otto, Count of Savoy"), the family of the [Counts of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") gained control. While the title of count was held by the Bishop as count of [Turin](/wiki/County_of_Turin "County of Turin") (1092–1130 and 1136–1191) it was ruled as a [prince-bishopric](/wiki/Prince-Bishop "Prince-Bishop") by the Bishops. In 1230–1235, it was a lordship under the [Marquess of Montferrat](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Montferrat "List of rulers of Montferrat"), styled Lord of Turin. At the end of the 13th century, when it was annexed to the Duchy of Savoy, the city already had 20,000 inhabitants. Many of the gardens and palaces were built in the 15th century when the city was redesigned. The [University of Turin](/wiki/University_of_Turin "University of Turin") was also founded during this period.

### Early modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early modern")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/%22S._Sindone_in_gloria_di_angeli%2C_il_Miracolo_Eucaristico%2C_i_Santi%22._Torino%2C_Giovanni_Boglietto%2C_1701.jpg/250px-%22S._Sindone_in_gloria_di_angeli%2C_il_Miracolo_Eucaristico%2C_i_Santi%22._Torino%2C_Giovanni_Boglietto%2C_1701.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22S._Sindone_in_gloria_di_angeli,_il_Miracolo_Eucaristico,_i_Santi%22._Torino,_Giovanni_Boglietto,_1701.jpg)

Turin in 1701

[Emmanuel Philibert](/wiki/Emmanuel_Philibert,_Duke_of_Savoy "Emmanuel Philibert, Duke of Savoy"), also known under the nickname of *Iron Head* (Testa 'd Fer), made Turin the capital of the Duchy of Savoy in 1563.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Piazza Reale (named [Piazza San Carlo](/wiki/Piazza_San_Carlo "Piazza San Carlo") today) and Via Nuova (current Via Roma) were added along with the first enlargement of the walls, in the first half of the 17th century; in the same period the *Palazzo Reale* ([Royal Palace of Turin](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Turin "Royal Palace of Turin")) was also built. In the second half of that century, a second enlargement of the walls was planned and executed, with the building of the arcaded Via Po, connecting Piazza Castello with the bridge on the Po through the regular street grid.

In 1706, during the [Battle of Turin](/wiki/Battle_of_Turin "Battle of Turin"), the French besieged the city for 117 days without conquering it. By the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") the Duke of Savoy acquired [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"), soon traded for [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia"), and part of the former [Duchy of Milan](/wiki/Duchy_of_Milan "Duchy of Milan"), and obtained the title of [King of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)"); thus Turin became the capital of a European kingdom. The architect [Filippo Juvarra](/wiki/Filippo_Juvarra "Filippo Juvarra") began a major redesign of the city; Turin had about 90,000 inhabitants at the time.

### Late modern and contemporary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Late modern and contemporary")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Maggi%2C_Giovanni_Battista_-_Torino_-_Panorama.jpg/250px-Maggi%2C_Giovanni_Battista_-_Torino_-_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maggi,_Giovanni_Battista_-_Torino_-_Panorama.jpg)

A view of Turin in the late 19th century. In the background, the [Mole Antonelliana](/wiki/Mole_Antonelliana "Mole Antonelliana") is under construction.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Fiat_Lingotto_veduta-1928.jpg/250px-Fiat_Lingotto_veduta-1928.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fiat_Lingotto_veduta-1928.jpg)

Fiat [Lingotto](/wiki/Lingotto "Lingotto") factory in 1928

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/6_maggio_1945_liberazione_torino.jpg/250px-6_maggio_1945_liberazione_torino.jpg)](/wiki/File:6_maggio_1945_liberazione_torino.jpg)

[Liberation of Italy](/wiki/Liberation_of_Italy "Liberation of Italy") parade in Turin on 6 May 1945.

Turin, like the rest of [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont"), was annexed by the [French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") in 1802. The city thus became the seat of the [prefecture](/wiki/Prefectures_in_France "Prefectures in France") of [Pô](/wiki/P%C3%B4_(department) "Pô (department)") [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") until the fall of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon_I "Napoleon I") in 1814, when the Kingdom of Piedmont-Sardinia was restored with Turin as its capital. In the following decades, the Kingdom of Piedmont-Sardinia led the struggle towards the [unification of Italy](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification"). In 1861, Turin became the capital of the newly proclaimed united [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(1861%E2%80%931946) "Kingdom of Italy (1861–1946)"), having been the political and intellectual centre of the *Risorgimento* movement.
[[8]](#cite_note-Britannica-10)[[25]](#cite_note-27) In 1865, the capital was moved to [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), and then to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") after the 1870 conquest of the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States").

The 1871 opening of the [Fréjus Tunnel](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9jus_Rail_Tunnel "Fréjus Rail Tunnel") made Turin an important communication node between Italy and France. The city in that period had 250,000 inhabitants. Some of the most iconic landmarks of the city, such as the [Mole Antonelliana](/wiki/Mole_Antonelliana "Mole Antonelliana"), the [Egyptian Museum](/wiki/Museo_Egizio "Museo Egizio"), the [Gran Madre di Dio church](/wiki/Gran_Madre_di_Dio,_Turin "Gran Madre di Dio, Turin") and *Piazza Vittorio Veneto*, were built in this period.

The late 19th century was also a period of rapid industrialisation, especially in the automotive sector: in 1899 [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat") was established in the city, followed by [Lancia](/wiki/Lancia "Lancia") in 1906. The [Universal Exposition](/wiki/Prima_Esposizione_Internazionale_d%27Arte_Decorativa_Moderna "Prima Esposizione Internazionale d'Arte Decorativa Moderna") held in Turin in 1902 is often regarded as the pinnacle of [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau") design, and the city hosted the same event in [1911](/wiki/Turin_International "Turin International"). By this time, Turin had grown to 430,000 inhabitants.

After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), harsh conditions brought a wave of strikes and workers' protests. In 1920 the [Lingotto](/wiki/Lingotto "Lingotto") Fiat factory was occupied. The [Fascist regime in Italy](/wiki/Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fascist regime in Italy") put an end to the social unrest, banning trade unions and jailing socialist leaders, notably [Antonio Gramsci](/wiki/Antonio_Gramsci "Antonio Gramsci"). On the other hand, [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") largely subsidised the automotive industry, in order to provide vehicles to the army.

Turin [was a target of Allied strategic bombing during World War II](/wiki/Bombing_of_Turin_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Turin in World War II"), being heavily damaged by the air raids in its industrial areas as well as in the city centre. Along with [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), and [La Spezia](/wiki/La_Spezia "La Spezia"), Turin was one of Italy's four cities that suffered [area bombing](/wiki/Area_bombing "Area bombing") by the RAF; the heaviest raid took place on 13 July 1943, when 295 bombers dropped 763 tons of bombs, killing 792 people.[[26]](#cite_note-28) Overall, these raids killed 2,069 inhabitants of Turin, and destroyed or damaged 54% of all buildings in the city.[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)

The Allies' [campaign in Italy](/wiki/Italian_Campaign_(World_War_II) "Italian Campaign (World War II)") started from the [south](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy") and slowly moved northwards in the following two years. The northern regions were occupied by Germans and collaborationist forces for several years. Turin was not captured by the Allies until the end of the [Spring Offensive of 1945](/wiki/Spring_1945_offensive_in_Italy "Spring 1945 offensive in Italy"). By the time the vanguard of the armoured reconnaissance units of the [Brazilian Expeditionary Force](/wiki/Brazilian_Expeditionary_Force "Brazilian Expeditionary Force") reached the city, it was already freed by the [Italian Partisans](/wiki/Italian_Partisans "Italian Partisans"). They had begun revolting against the Germans and the Italian RSI troops on 25 April 1945. Days later, troops from the US Army's [1st Armored](/wiki/1st_Armored_Division_(United_States) "1st Armored Division (United States)") and [92nd Infantry Divisions](/wiki/92nd_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "92nd Infantry Division (United States)") came to substitute for the Brazilians.[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32)

In the postwar years, Turin was rapidly rebuilt. The city's automotive industry played a pivotal role in the [Italian economic miracle](/wiki/Italian_economic_miracle "Italian economic miracle") of the 1950s and 1960s, attracting hundreds of thousands of migrants to the city, particularly from the rural southern regions of Italy. The number of migrants was so high that Turin was said to be "the third southern Italian city after [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") and [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo")". The population soon reached 1 million in 1960 and peaked at almost 1.2 million in 1971. The exceptional growth gains of the city gained it the nickname of *Capitale dell'automobile* (Automobile Capital), being often compared with [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), the major centre of the [U.S. automobile industry](/wiki/U.S._automobile_industry "U.S. automobile industry") (these cities were 'twinned' as sister cities in 1998).

In the 1970s and 1980s, the oil and automotive industry crisis severely hit the city, and its population began to sharply decline as jobs were lost. In 30 years, the population decreased by more than one-fourth of the 1971 total. The long population decline of the city has begun to reverse itself only in recent years; the population grew from 865,000 to slightly over 900,000 by the end of the 20th century. In 2006, Turin hosted the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/142957main_iss012e15598_torino.jpg/250px-142957main_iss012e15598_torino.jpg)](/wiki/File:142957main_iss012e15598_torino.jpg)

Turin from space. North is on the left.

Turin is in [northwest Italy](/wiki/Northwest_Italy "Northwest Italy"). It is surrounded on the western and northern front by the Alps and on the eastern front by a high hill that is the natural continuation of the hills of [Monferrato](/wiki/Montferrat "Montferrat"). Four major rivers pass through the city: the Po and three of its tributaries, the [River Dora Riparia](/wiki/Dora_Riparia "Dora Riparia") (once known as *Duria Minor* by the Romans, from the [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") noun *duria*, 'water'), the [Stura di Lanzo](/wiki/Stura_di_Lanzo "Stura di Lanzo") and the [Sangone](/wiki/Sangone_(torrent) "Sangone (torrent)").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

Located in northwestern Italy at the foot of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"), Turin features a mid-latitude, four seasons [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfa*), similar to that of [Grenoble](/wiki/Grenoble "Grenoble"), located not far away in the French Alps.

Winters are moderately cold and dry; summers are mild in the hills and quite hot in the plains. Rain falls mostly during spring and autumn; during the hottest months, otherwise, rains are less frequent but heavier (thunderstorms are frequent). During the winter and autumn months banks of fog, which are sometimes very thick, form in the plains[[31]](#cite_note-33) but rarely on the city because of its location at the end of the [Susa Valley](/wiki/Susa_Valley "Susa Valley"). Snowfalls are not uncommon during the winter months, though substantial accumulation is quite uncommon.

Its position on the east side of the Alps makes the weather drier than on the west side because of the [föhn wind](/wiki/F%C3%B6hn_wind "Föhn wind") effect.

The highest temperature ever recorded was 37.1 °C (98.8 °F) on [11 August 2003](/wiki/2003_European_heat_wave "2003 European heat wave"), and the lowest was −21.8 °C (−7.2 °F) on 12 February 1956.

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Administration")]

See also: [List of mayors of Turin](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Turin "List of mayors of Turin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Circoscrizioni_torino_2016.png/500px-Circoscrizioni_torino_2016.png)](/wiki/File:Circoscrizioni_torino_2016.png)

Administrative map of Turin

Turin is split up into 8 [boroughs](/wiki/Borough "Borough"), locally called *[circoscrizioni](/wiki/Circoscrizione "Circoscrizione")*;[[40]](#cite_note-circoscrizioni-42) these do not necessarily correspond to the historical districts of the city, which are rather called *[quartieri](/wiki/Quartiere "Quartiere")*, *[rioni](/wiki/Rione "Rione")*, *borghi*, *borgate* or *zone*. The *circoscrizioni* system originally comprised 10 of them, that were reduced to 8 by merging borough 9 into 8, and 10 into 2.

The following list enumerates the boroughs and the location of the historical districts inside them:[[40]](#cite_note-circoscrizioni-42)

* Circoscrizione 1: Centro – Crocetta
* Circoscrizione 2: Santa Rita – Mirafiori Nord – Mirafiori Sud
* Circoscrizione 3: San Paolo – Cenisia – Pozzo Strada – Cit Turin – Borgata Lesna
* Circoscrizione 4: San Donato – Campidoglio – Parella
* Circoscrizione 5: Borgo Vittoria – Madonna di Campagna – Lucento – Vallette
* Circoscrizione 6: Barriera di Milano – Regio Parco – Barca – Bertolla – Falchera – Rebaudengo – Villaretto
* Circoscrizione 7: [Aurora](/wiki/Aurora_(Turin) "Aurora (Turin)") – Vanchiglia – Sassi – Madonna del Pilone
* Circoscrizione 8: San Salvario – Cavoretto – Borgo Po – Nizza Millefonti – [Lingotto](/wiki/Lingotto_(neighbourhood) "Lingotto (neighbourhood)") – Filadelfia

The mayor of Turin is directly elected every five years. The current mayor of the city is [Stefano Lo Russo](/wiki/Stefano_Lo_Russo "Stefano Lo Russo") ([PD](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Italy) "Democratic Party (Italy)")), elected in 2021.

## Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

### City centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: City centre")]

See also: [Residences of the Royal House of Savoy](/wiki/Residences_of_the_Royal_House_of_Savoy "Residences of the Royal House of Savoy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Torino%2C_via_Roma_%2801%29.jpg/250px-Torino%2C_via_Roma_%2801%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torino,_via_Roma_(01).jpg)

*Via Roma*

Turin's historical architecture is predominantly [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") and was developed under the [Savoyard state](/wiki/Savoyard_state "Savoyard state"). Nonetheless, the main street of the city centre, *Via Roma*, was built during the Fascist era (from 1931 to 1937) as an example of [Italian Rationalism](/wiki/Italian_Rationalism "Italian Rationalism"), replacing former buildings already present in this area.

Via Roma runs between [Piazza Carlo Felice](/wiki/Piazza_Carlo_Felice "Piazza Carlo Felice") and [Piazza Castello](/wiki/Piazza_Castello "Piazza Castello"). Buildings on the portion between Piazza Carlo Felice and [Piazza San Carlo](/wiki/Piazza_San_Carlo "Piazza San Carlo") were designed by rationalist architect [Marcello Piacentini](/wiki/Marcello_Piacentini "Marcello Piacentini"). These blocks were built into a reticular system, composed by austere buildings in clear rationalist style, such as the impressive *Hotel Principi di Piemonte* and the former *Hotel Nazionale* in *Piazza CLN*. Porches are built in a continuous [entablature](/wiki/Entablature "Entablature") and marked with double columns, to be consistent with those of Piazza San Carlo. The section of the street between Piazza San Carlo and Piazza Castello was built in an eclectic style, with arcades characterised by [Serliana](/wiki/Serliana "Serliana")-type arches. To this day Via Roma is the street featuring the most fashionable boutiques of the city.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Torino_-_Caval_%C3%ABd_Brons_latoB.jpg/250px-Torino_-_Caval_%C3%ABd_Brons_latoB.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torino_-_Caval_%C3%ABd_Brons_latoB.jpg)

*Piazza San Carlo* and the *Caval 'd Brons* (Bronze Horse in Piedmontese language), equestrian monument to [Emmanuel Philibert](/wiki/Emanuele_Filiberto,_Duke_of_Savoy "Emanuele Filiberto, Duke of Savoy")

Via Roma crosses one of the main squares of the city: the pedestrianised Piazza San Carlo, built by [Carlo di Castellamonte](/wiki/Carlo_di_Castellamonte "Carlo di Castellamonte") in the 17th century. In the middle of the square stands the equestrian monument to [Emmanuel Philibert](/wiki/Emanuele_Filiberto,_Duke_of_Savoy "Emanuele Filiberto, Duke of Savoy"), also known as *Caval ëd Brons* in the local dialect ('Bronze Horse'); the monument depicts the Duke sheathing his sword after the [Battle of St. Quentin](/wiki/Battle_of_St._Quentin_(1557) "Battle of St. Quentin (1557)"). Piazza San Carlo arcades host the most ancient cafés of the city, such as *Caffé Torino* and *Caffé San Carlo*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Torino_-_Palazzo_Reale_-_1.jpg/250px-Torino_-_Palazzo_Reale_-_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torino_-_Palazzo_Reale_-_1.jpg)

*Piazza Castello* with [Palazzo Reale](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Turin "Royal Palace of Turin") (Royal Palace) in the background

At the northern end of Via Roma stands *Piazza Castello*, regarded as the heart of the city. The half-pedestrianised square hosts some significant buildings such as *Palazzo Reale* (Former Savoy Royal House), the *Palazzo Madama* (which previously hosted the Savoy senate and, for few years, the Italian senate after Italian unification), the former Baroque [Teatro Regio di Torino](/wiki/Teatro_Regio_di_Torino "Teatro Regio di Torino") (rebuilt in modern style in the 1960s, after being destroyed by fire), the [Royal Library of Turin](/wiki/Royal_Library_of_Turin "Royal Library of Turin") which hosts the [Leonardo da Vinci self-portrait](/wiki/Portrait_of_a_man_in_red_chalk_(Leonardo) "Portrait of a man in red chalk (Leonardo)"), and the baroque [Royal Church of San Lorenzo](/wiki/San_Lorenzo,_Turin "San Lorenzo, Turin"). Moreover, Piazza Castello hosts a Fascist era building, the [Torre Littoria](/wiki/Torre_Littoria "Torre Littoria"), a sort of skyscraper which was supposed to become the headquarters of the Fascist party, although it never served as such. The building's style is quite different from the Baroque style of Piazza Castello. The square regularly hosts the main open space events of the city, live concerts included.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/11/Porta_Nuova_Station_Turin.jpg/250px-Porta_Nuova_Station_Turin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Porta_Nuova_Station_Turin.jpg)

*Porta Nuova* main railway station

As for the southern part of the street, Via Roma ends in *Piazza Carlo Felice* and in its *Giardino Sambuy*, a wide fenced garden right in the middle of the square. Across from Piazza Carlo Felice stands the monumental façade of [Porta Nuova railway station](/wiki/Torino_Porta_Nuova_railway_station "Torino Porta Nuova railway station"), the central station of the city built between 1861 and 1868 by the architect Alessandro Mazzucchetti. The passengers building was renovated to host a shopping mall and more efficient passenger service offices. However, it is still an example of monumental architecture, with its stately foyer and some Baroque sights, such as the *Sala Reale* (the former Royal waiting room).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Torino%2C_piazza_Vittorio_Veneto_%2804%29.jpg/250px-Torino%2C_piazza_Vittorio_Veneto_%2804%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torino,_piazza_Vittorio_Veneto_(04).jpg)

*Piazza Vittorio Veneto* square

In *Piazza Castello* converge some of the main streets of the city centre. Among them, one of the most significant is the arcaded *Via Po*, built by [Amedeo di Castellamonte](/wiki/Amedeo_di_Castellamonte "Amedeo di Castellamonte") in 1674 and featuring some interesting buildings, such as the first and original building of the [University of Turin](/wiki/University_of_Turin "University of Turin") and the historical [Caffè Fiorio](/wiki/Caff%C3%A8_Fiorio "Caffè Fiorio"), which was the favourite café of the 19th-century politicians. Via Po ends in [Piazza Vittorio Veneto](/wiki/Piazza_Vittorio_Veneto "Piazza Vittorio Veneto") (simply called Piazza Vittorio locally), the largest Baroque square in Europe and today heart of Turin nightlife. Piazza Vittorio features the most fashionable bars and not far from here, along the [Po](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)") riverfront, the *Murazzi* quays used to host several bars and nightclubs open until the morning until a few years ago.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Piazza_and_Palazzo_Carignano_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Piazza_and_Palazzo_Carignano_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piazza_and_Palazzo_Carignano_-_panoramio.jpg)

Baroque façade of [Palazzo Carignano](/wiki/Palazzo_Carignano "Palazzo Carignano"), the [Museum of the Risorgimento](/wiki/Museum_of_the_Risorgimento_(Turin) "Museum of the Risorgimento (Turin)")

Parallel to Via Roma, the other two popular pedestrian streets, namely *Via Lagrange* and *Via Carlo Alberto*, cross the old town from Via Po to *Corso Vittorio Emanuele II*. Their recent pedestrianisation has improved their original commercial vocation. In particular, Via Lagrange has recently increased the presence of luxury boutiques. This street also hosts the [Egyptian Museum of Turin](/wiki/Museo_Egizio "Museo Egizio"), home to what is regarded as one of the largest collections of Egyptian antiquities outside of Egypt.

Via Lagrange and Via Carlo Alberto cross two significant squares of the city, respectively. The former crosses *Piazza Carignano*, well known mainly for the undulating "concave – convex-concave" Baroque façade of [Palazzo Carignano](/wiki/Palazzo_Carignano "Palazzo Carignano"). This building used to host the *Parlamento Subalpino* (the 'Subalpine Parliament', [Parliament of the Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Kingdom_of_Sardinia "Parliament of the Kingdom of Sardinia") which also became the Italian Parliament for a few years, after the Italian unification) and today houses the [Museum of the Risorgimento](/wiki/Museum_of_the_Risorgimento_(Turin) "Museum of the Risorgimento (Turin)"). The square also features the [Teatro Carignano](/wiki/Teatro_Carignano "Teatro Carignano"), a well-conserved Baroque theatre. Via Carlo Alberto crosses *Piazza Carlo Alberto*, a big square hosting the rear façade of Palazzo Carignano, in eclectic style. On the other side stands the monumental *Biblioteca Nazionale* (National Library).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Torino-mole10.jpg/250px-Torino-mole10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torino-mole10.jpg)

[Mole Antonelliana](/wiki/Mole_Antonelliana "Mole Antonelliana") in 2006

Not far from Via Po stands the symbol of Turin, namely the [Mole Antonelliana](/wiki/Mole_Antonelliana "Mole Antonelliana"), so named after the architect who built it, [Alessandro Antonelli](/wiki/Alessandro_Antonelli "Alessandro Antonelli"). Construction began in 1863 as a Jewish [synagogue](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue"). Nowadays it houses the [National Museum of Cinema](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Cinema "National Museum of Cinema") and it is believed to be the tallest museum in the world at 167 m (548 ft). The building is depicted on the Italian 2-cent coin.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Chapel_of_Holy_Shroud_Cupola.jpg/250px-Chapel_of_Holy_Shroud_Cupola.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chapel_of_Holy_Shroud_Cupola.jpg)

Dome of [Turin Cathedral](/wiki/Turin_Cathedral "Turin Cathedral")

Just behind *Piazza Castello* stands the [Turin Cathedral](/wiki/Turin_Cathedral "Turin Cathedral"), dedicated to Saint [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"), which is the major church of the city. It was built during 1491–1498 and is adjacent to an earlier [bell tower](/wiki/Bell_tower "Bell tower") (1470). Annexed to the cathedral is the [Chapel of the Holy Shroud](/wiki/Chapel_of_the_Holy_Shroud "Chapel of the Holy Shroud"), the current resting place of the [Shroud of Turin](/wiki/Shroud_of_Turin "Shroud of Turin"). The chapel was added to the structure in 1668–1694, designed by [Guarini](/wiki/Guarino_Guarini "Guarino Guarini"). The [Basilica of Corpus Domini](/wiki/Basilica_of_Corpus_Domini "Basilica of Corpus Domini") was built to celebrate an alleged miracle which took place during the sack of the city in 1453, when a soldier was carrying off a [monstrance](/wiki/Monstrance "Monstrance") containing the [Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Blessed_Sacrament "Blessed Sacrament"); the monstrance fell to the ground, while the [host](/wiki/Sacramental_bread "Sacramental bread") remained suspended in air. The present church, erected in 1610 to replace the original chapel which stood on the spot, is the work of [Ascanio Vitozzi](/wiki/Ascanio_Vitozzi "Ascanio Vitozzi").

Next to the Turin Cathedral stand the [Palatine Towers](/wiki/Palatine_Towers "Palatine Towers"), an ancient [Roman](/wiki/Architecture_of_ancient_Rome "Architecture of ancient Rome")-medieval structure that served as one of four Roman [city gates](/wiki/City_gate "City gate") along the [city walls](/wiki/City_walls "City walls") of Turin. This gate allowed access from north to the *[cardo](/wiki/Cardo "Cardo") maximus*, the typical second main street of a Roman town. The Palatine Towers are among the best preserved Roman remains in northern Italy. Close to this site, the 51,300 m2 (552,189 sq ft) *Piazza della Repubblica* plays host to the biggest open market in Europe, locally known as *mercato di Porta Palazzo* (*Porta Palazzo* or *Porta Pila* are the historical and local names of this area).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Torino_-_Porta_Palatina.jpg/250px-Torino_-_Porta_Palatina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torino_-_Porta_Palatina.jpg)

[Palatine Towers](/wiki/Palatine_Towers "Palatine Towers")

West of the Porte Palatine stands the *Quadrilatero Romano* (Roman Quadrilateral), the old medieval district recently renewed. The current neighbourhood is characterised by its tiny streets and its several medieval buildings and today it is popular for its *[aperitivo](/wiki/Italian_meal_structure#Formal_meal_structure "Italian meal structure")* bars and its small shops run by local artisans. The hub of the Quadrilatero is *Piazza Emanuele Filiberto*.

South of the Quadrilatero Romano stands *Via Garibaldi*, another popular street of the city. It is a 1 km (0.6 mi) pedestrian street between Piazza Castello and *Piazza Statuto* which features some of the old shops of the city. Large *Piazza Statuto* is another example of Baroque square with arcades.

Another main street of downtown is *Via Pietro Micca*, which starts in Piazza Castello and ends in the large *Piazza Solferino*. The street continues in *Via Cernaia* up to *Piazza XVIII Dicembre*, which features the former [Porta Susa](/wiki/Porta_Susa "Porta Susa") passengers building, relocated in 2012 a little more southward. The new and larger passengers building is situated between *Corso Bolzano* and *Corso Inghilterra* and is an example of contemporary architecture, being a 300 m-long (980 ft) and 19 m-high (62 ft) glass and steel structure. Porta Susa is currently the international central station of the city (high speed trains to Paris) and it is becoming the central hub of railway transportation of the city, being the station in which local trains (so-called *Ferrovie Metropolitane*), national trains and high-speed national and international trains converge.

Close to Via Cernaia stands the *Cittadella* (Citadel), in the *Andrea Guglielminetti garden*. What remains of the old medieval and modern fortress of the city, it is a starting point for a tour into the old tunnels below the city.

### San Salvario

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: San Salvario")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Borgo_Medievale%2C_Torino.jpg/250px-Borgo_Medievale%2C_Torino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Borgo_Medievale,_Torino.jpg)

*[Borgo Medioevale](/wiki/Borgo_Medioevale "Borgo Medioevale")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Valentino_castle.jpg/250px-Valentino_castle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valentino_castle.jpg)

[Castello del Valentino](/wiki/Castello_del_Valentino "Castello del Valentino") in [Parco del Valentino](/wiki/Parco_del_Valentino "Parco del Valentino")

Southeast of the city centre stands *San Salvario* district, which extends from *Corso Vittorio Emanuele II* to *Corso Bramante* and is delimited by the [Turin-Genoa railway](/wiki/Turin-Genoa_railway "Turin-Genoa railway") on the west side and by the [River Po](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)") on the east side. Home to an increasing immigrants' community, the district is an example of integration among different cultures; it also features an incremented nightlife after the opening of several low-cost bars and restaurants.

San Salvario is crossed by two main roads, *Via Nizza* and *Via Madama Cristina*, and just as the city centre it is characterised by the [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan") typical of Turin's old neighbourhoods. The hub of the district is *Piazza Madama Cristina* which hosts a big open market, while several commercial activities flourish around it.

The celebrated [Parco del Valentino](/wiki/Parco_del_Valentino "Parco del Valentino") is situated in the east side of San Salvario and, albeit not in downtown, it represents a type of central park of Turin. Thanks to the vicinity to the city centre, the park is very popular among the local people, during the day but also at night, because of the several bars and nightclubs placed here. From the terraces of Parco del Valentino, many sights of the hills on the other side of the river can be appreciated.

In the centre of the park stands the [Castello del Valentino](/wiki/Castello_del_Valentino "Castello del Valentino"), built in the 17th century. This castle has a horseshoe shape, with four rectangular towers, one at each angle, and a wide inner court with a marble pavement. The ceilings of the false upper floors are in *transalpino* (i.e. French) style. The façade sports the huge coat of arms of the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"). Today, Castello del Valentino serves as the faculty of Architecture of the *[Polytechnic University of Turin](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Turin "Polytechnic University of Turin")*.

Another cluster of buildings in the park is the *[Borgo Medioevale](/wiki/Borgo_Medioevale "Borgo Medioevale")* (Medieval village), a replica of medieval mountain castles of Piedmont and [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley"), built for the 1884 [International Exhibition](/wiki/World%27s_fair "World's fair").

Other buildings in *Corso Massimo d'Azeglio* include the [Torino Esposizioni](/wiki/Torino_Esposizioni "Torino Esposizioni") complex (Turin's exhibition hall built in the 1930s) featuring a monumental entrance with a large full height porch, a main hall designed by [Pier Luigi Nervi](/wiki/Pier_Luigi_Nervi "Pier Luigi Nervi") in reinforced concrete, and the *Teatro Nuovo*, a theatre mostly focused on ballet exhibitions. Another building is the largest synagogue of the city, in *Piazzetta Primo Levi*, a square. Its architecture stands in the main sight of the city, as characterised by four large towers—27 m (89 ft) high—topped by four onion-shaped domes.

### Crocetta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Crocetta")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Vittorio_Emanuele_II_-_panoramio_%282%29.jpg/250px-Vittorio_Emanuele_II_-_panoramio_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vittorio_Emanuele_II_-_panoramio_(2).jpg)

*Monumento a Vittorio Emanuele II*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Grattacielo_Sanpaolo_e_Monte_Rosa_da_Torino.jpg/250px-Grattacielo_Sanpaolo_e_Monte_Rosa_da_Torino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grattacielo_Sanpaolo_e_Monte_Rosa_da_Torino.jpg)

The [Polytechnic University of Turin](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Turin "Polytechnic University of Turin")

South of *Centro* stands the *Crocetta* district, considered one of the most exclusive districts of the city, because of highly rated residential buildings. At the heart of the district is the partially pedestrianised area crossed by *Corso Trieste*, *Corso Trento* and *Corso Duca D'Aosta*, plenty of some notable residential buildings in [eclectic](/wiki/Eclectic_architecture "Eclectic architecture"), [neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-Gothic_architecture "Neo-Gothic architecture") and [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau_architecture "Art Nouveau architecture") style. The area was built between 1903 and 1937 replacing the old [parade ground](/wiki/Parade_ground "Parade ground"), which was moved in the southern part of the city.

North of this area stands the *GAM (Galleria d'Arte Moderna)*, one of the two Museum of Modern Arts of the Turin Metro area (the second and largest one is hosted in *Castello di Rivoli*, a former Savoy royal castle in the suburbs). The museum stands in front a huge monument situated in the centre of the roundabout between *Corso Vittorio Emanuele II* and *Corso Galileo Ferraris*: the [Monument to Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Monument_to_Vittorio_Emanuele_II_(Turin) "Monument to Vittorio Emanuele II (Turin)"), a king of Savoy statue situated on a 39-metres high column. Next to the museum, another significant residential building previously hosted the head office of [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus "Juventus"), one of the two main Turin [football clubs](/wiki/Football_clubs "Football clubs").

West of this area, the main building of [Polytechnic University of Turin](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Turin "Polytechnic University of Turin") stands along *Corso Duca Degli Abruzzi*. The 1958 building, a 122,000 m2 (1,313,197 sq ft) complex, hosts approximately 30,000 students and is considered one of the major institutes of technology of the country—mainly due to the vocation of the city for the industrialisation, pushed by the automotive sector. This institute recently expanded in the western district of *Cenisia* with additional modern buildings.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Spina_2_020406_051.jpg/250px-Spina_2_020406_051.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spina_2_020406_051.jpg)

The *Spina Centrale* is a new under-construction boulevard built over the undergrounded railway. It is already completed in *Crocetta*.

Crocetta is crossed by large and modern avenues, such as *Corso Duca degli Abruzzi*, *Corso Galileo Ferraris*, and *Corso Einaudi*. These avenues feature long rows of trees, symbolic of Turin's typical urbanity. However, the most popular avenue is *Corso De Gasperi*, which, albeit smaller than other avenues of the district, hosts one of the most fashionable open markets of the city, the so-called *Mercato della Crocetta*, in which it is possible to find some discounted branded clothing among the more popular ones.

The Western border of Crocetta is instead an example of contemporary architecture. The huge avenue, made up of *Corso Mediterraneo* and *Corso Castelfidardo*, is part of *Spina Centrale* boulevard and was recently built over the old railway (now undergrounded): as a result, the avenue is very large (up to 60 m (200 ft)) and modern, having been rebuilt with valuable materials, including a characteristic lighting system supported by white high poles. This avenue hosts some examples of contemporary art, such as [Mario Merz](/wiki/Mario_Merz "Mario Merz")'s *Igloo* fountain or the [Per Kirkeby](/wiki/Per_Kirkeby "Per Kirkeby")'s *Opera per Torino* monument in *Largo Orbassano*.

The East side of the district is also known as *Borgo San Secondo* named after the church of the same name standing in *Via San Secondo*, a major street in the neighbourhood. This is near *Porta Nuova* railway station and is older than the rest of the district, featuring several apartment buildings from the late 19th century, to include the birthplace and home of author [Primo Levi](/wiki/Primo_Levi "Primo Levi") on Corso Re Umberto.[[41]](#cite_note-43) A local open market is held in *Piazza San Secondo* and along *Via Legnano*. The market square also hosts the former washhouse and public baths of the neighbourhood, among the oldest examples of their kind in Turin (1905).

One of the main thoroughfares crossing Borgo San Secondo is *Via Sacchi*, which serves as an ideal gate to the city centre: its Serlian arcades on the west side of the street (the east side is enclosed by *Porta Nuova* railway station service buildings) host some significant boutiques and hotels, such as the historic *Pfatisch* pastry shop and the *Turin Palace Hotel* (totally refurbished and reopened in 2015). South of *Via Sacchi*, *Ospedale Mauriziano* is one of the ancient and major hospitals of the city. Going further southwards, it is possible to appreciate an interesting residential cluster of old public housing gravitating around *Via Arquata*.

### Cenisia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Cenisia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/PiazzaCarloAlbertoTorino.jpg/250px-PiazzaCarloAlbertoTorino.jpg)](/wiki/File:PiazzaCarloAlbertoTorino.jpg)

The Piazza Carlo Alberto

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Heating_Power_Plant_Polito.jpg/250px-Heating_Power_Plant_Polito.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heating_Power_Plant_Polito.jpg)

A heating [power plant](/wiki/Power_plant "Power plant")

Bordered by *Corso Castelfidardo*, *Corso Vittorio Emanuele II*, *Corso Trapani* and *Corso Peschiera*, this small district is mainly significant for hosting the recent expansion of Turinese [institute of technology](/wiki/Institute_of_technology "Institute of technology") *[Politecnico](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Turin "Polytechnic University of Turin")*. The expansion was possible after under-grounding the railway under *Corso Castelfidardo* and the subsequent disposal of the old buildings dedicated to the train maintenance present in this area (so-called *Officine Grandi Riparazioni* or *OGR*). *Politecnico* expanded its facilities through two huge overpass buildings over the avenue, linked to new buildings on the west side. This cluster of buildings forms an evocative square with a unique architectural style. The main building on the west side hosts a [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") research centre, the *General Motors Global Propulsion Systems* (formerly known as *General Motors Powertrain Europe*). *Politecnico* area extends until *Via Boggio* with further facilities hosted in the former *OGR* facilities. The institute plans to further build new facilities in the current parking area.

North of *Politecnico* facilities, the main building of the *OGR* former cluster, which consists in three 180-metres long joint parallel buildings, became recently a big open space which hosts temporary exhibitions and during the hot seasons, its external spaces became a fashionable site to have a typical Italian *[aperitivo](/wiki/Italian_meal_structure#Formal_meal_structure "Italian meal structure")*.

North of *OGR*, a former prison complex called *Le Nuove* is a significant example of old European prison building. The complex was built between 1857 and 1869 during the reign of Victor Emmanuel II. After being disposed of during the 1990s, the complex was changed into a museum and it is possible to visit its facilities.

An example of contemporary art is the heating plant in *Corso Ferrucci*, which has been covered with aluminium panels. Another building (19th century), now abandoned, is the former [Westinghouse](/wiki/Westinghouse_Brakes#Rail_vehicles "Westinghouse Brakes") factory of train brakes situated in *Via Borsellino*.

The residential and business zone of the district sprawls westward, beyond the former—now demolished—customs wall (*cinta daziaria*), which previously separated the city from the mainly rural landscape that marked the outskirts of Turin until the late 19th century. Urban planning outside the local [city gate](/wiki/City_gate "City gate") (so-called *barriera di San Paolo*) led to the construction of an industrial and working-class neighbourhood in the early 20th century, although factories have long been discontinued, torn down or converted to other uses nowadays. Together with *San Paolo* district, *Cenisia* hosts an extensive [street market](/wiki/Street_market "Street market") along *Corso Racconigi*, which is locally known as the longest street market in Europe.

### Cit Turin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Cit Turin")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Palazzo_della_Vittoria_-_panoramio_%283%29.jpg/250px-Palazzo_della_Vittoria_-_panoramio_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palazzo_della_Vittoria_-_panoramio_(3).jpg)

*Casa della Vittoria* (1918–1920) is an example of Turin's neo-gothic architecture.

The smallest district of the city is *Cit Turin* ('Little Turin' in [Piedmontese language](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language")). This small triangle surrounded by *Corso Vittorio Emanuele II*, *Corso Francia* and *Corso Inghilterra* hosts some high rated residential buildings and is regarded as a prestigious residential neighbourhood by local people.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Grattacielo_Intesa_San_Paolo_-_panoramio_%283%29.jpg/250px-Grattacielo_Intesa_San_Paolo_-_panoramio_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grattacielo_Intesa_San_Paolo_-_panoramio_(3).jpg)

[Intesa Sanpaolo](/wiki/Intesa_Sanpaolo "Intesa Sanpaolo") banking group headquarters

The district features many buildings in [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau"), [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") and [Neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-Gothic "Neo-Gothic") style. Among them, one of the most impressive and well known is the *[Casa della Vittoria](/wiki/Casa_della_Vittoria "Casa della Vittoria")* (architect *Gottardo Gussoni*). Another notable example is [Casa Fenoglio-Lafleur](/wiki/Casa_Fenoglio-Lafleur "Casa Fenoglio-Lafleur"). Both buildings face Corso Francia.

The district is well known for its commercial vocation mainly in its two main streets, *Via Duchessa Jolanda* and *Via Principi d'Acaja*, ideally crossing each other among the gardens *Giardino Luigi Martini*, locally called *Piazza Benefica*, which hosts a popular open market.

The district is also characterised by two massive recent buildings: the *Palazzo di Giustizia*, Turin's new courthouse built in the 1990s (in a 350-metre-long facility), and the first real skyscraper of Turin, the [Torre Intesa Sanpaolo](/wiki/Torre_Intesa_Sanpaolo "Torre Intesa Sanpaolo"),[[42]](#cite_note-44) which house the headquarters of one of the major Italian private banks.

### San Donato

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: San Donato")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Torino-CampanileChiesaDiNostraSignoradelSuffragio.png/250px-Torino-CampanileChiesaDiNostraSignoradelSuffragio.png)](/wiki/File:Torino-CampanileChiesaDiNostraSignoradelSuffragio.png)

*Chiesa di Nostra Signora del Suffragio e Santa Zita*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Torre_littoria_Torino.jpg/250px-Torre_littoria_Torino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torre_littoria_Torino.jpg)

The [Torre Littoria](/wiki/Torre_Littoria "Torre Littoria")

*San Donato* district is between *Corso Francia*, *Corso Lecce*, *Corso Potenza*, *Via Nole*, the *Parco Dora* and *Corso Principe Oddone*. It was populated since the medieval era, but becomes bigger during the 19th century, prospering around the canal *Canale di San Donato*, which does not exist anymore, currently replaced by the central street of the district, *Via San Donato*. Buildings in the district are relatively recent (around 1820), except for the oldest group of small houses in the *Brusachœr* neighbourhood (*Palazzo Forneris* building) along *Via Pacinotti* near the small *Piazza Paravia*. The conservation of the street and of this old building influences the straightness of *Via San Donato*, which makes a slight curve to result in parallel with *Via Pacinotti* before ending in central *Piazza Statuto* square.

Main church of the district is the *Chiesa di Nostra Signora del Suffragio e Santa Zita*, which with its 83 m (272 ft) height of its bell tower, is well known to be the fifth tallest structure in the city of Turin, after the [Mole Antonelliana](/wiki/Mole_Antonelliana "Mole Antonelliana"), the [Intesa-Sanpaolo skyscraper](/wiki/Intesa_Sanpaolo "Intesa Sanpaolo"), the [Torre Littoria](/wiki/Torre_Littoria "Torre Littoria") and the two pennons of the [Juventus Stadium](/wiki/Juventus_Stadium "Juventus Stadium"). The church is hosting the *Istituto Suore Minime di Nostra Signora del Suffragio* and it was promoted and designed by [Francesco Faà di Bruno](/wiki/Francesco_Fa%C3%A0_di_Bruno "Francesco Faà di Bruno"). The legend says, that he wanted to build the tallest bell tower of the town and put a clock on the top, to all the poor people to know the time for free. The small building near the church is what remains of *Casa Tartaglino*, a small residential building which was also extended and modified by Faa di Bruno.

*Villino Cibrario* in *Via Saccarelli* is another significant building designed by Barnaba Panizza in 1842. The building was equipped with a large garden which was eliminated to host the street. The neighbourhood has a high concentration of historic buildings in Art Nouveau style designed by architect Pietro Fenoglio (among the others, the prestigious *Villino Raby* in Corso Francia 8). Other significant buildings are the *Villa Boringhieri* in Via San Donato, and other Art Nouveau and Neo-Gothic buildings are situated in *Via Piffetti* and *Via Durandi*.

Among the modern buildings of the district, the most significant one is, of course, the *Torre BBPR* Tower (which took the name from the architecture office who designed it). The building is representing the *post-rationalism Italian architecture* (same style of the better known [Torre Velasca](/wiki/Torre_Velasca "Torre Velasca") tower in the city of Milan). The tower is facing the central *Piazza Statuto* square. The district is crossed by some significant avenues: on *Corso Svizzera*, which crosses the district from north to south, faces the Business Centre *Piero Della Francesca*, where the offices of *Tuttosport*, one of the three national sports daily newspapers has its head offices. Also on *Corso Svizzera*, stands one of the oldest hospitals of the city, the *Ospedale Amedeo di Savoia*, specialised in infectious diseases. Other major avenues are *Corso Umbria* and *Corso Tassoni*.

Another big avenue, which borders the district on its East, is *Corso Principe Oddone*, which in the past was along the railway to Milan. Currently the railway has been under-grounded: the avenue will be enlarged and have same architecture style of southern *Corso Inghilterra* in downtown, becoming one of the major avenues of Turin. The northern part of the district was part of the former industrial district of Turin, recently reconverted to a park called *Parco Dora*. Mainly, in *San Donato* the portion reconverted was the one occupied by the plant of [Michelin](/wiki/Michelin "Michelin") (west of *Via Livorno*) and [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat") ironwork plants (on the East). Differently for other portions of *Parco Dora*, this part has been totally reconverted to park without letting any evidence of the industrial area except for the cooling tower which stands along *Corso Umbria* and became a symbol of the park. Works are completed in the western area, where *Corso Mortara* has been closed to traffic and moved just a bit northern and covered by an artificial tunnel. It is possible to access the southern shore of the River Dora. South of the park, an interesting architecture of different levels is hosting a new shopping mall called *Centro Commerciale Parco Dora*. East of *Via Livorno*, works are still partially in progress, with the River Dora still to be uncovered by a big slab, on which the [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat") plants used to stand). West of *Via Livorno*, the *Environment Park* is a research centre for renewable energy.

### Aurora

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Aurora")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/BorgoDora.JPG/250px-BorgoDora.JPG)](/wiki/File:BorgoDora.JPG)

Tiny streets of *Borgo Dora*

*Aurora* is one of the most ancient districts which developed out of the medieval [city walls](/wiki/Defensive_wall "Defensive wall"), north of the historical city centre. It stretches from downtown northern boundaries in *Corso Regina Margherita* (an extended and important thoroughfare of Turin) up to *Corso Vigevano* and *Corso Novara* in the North Side (namely the old [excise](/wiki/Excise "Excise") boundary until the early 20th century); the western boundary is *Corso Principe Oddone* (now part of the *Spina Centrale* boulevard) and the eastern border is the River Dora.

The district was named Aurora after the so-called *Cascina Aurora*, an old farmstead lying north of the River Dora, right at the intersection between *Corso Giulio Cesare* and *Corso Emilia*. The farmstead has long been demolished and the area has been converted to office buildings, hosting the Turinese textile company *Gruppo Finanziario Tessile* (*GFT*) headquarters until the early 21st century.

The historical hub of the district is *Borgo Dora* (The 'Dora Borough'), a small neighbourhood next to *Porta Palazzo* and enclosed by *Corso Regina Margherita*, *Via Cigna*, the River Dora and *Corso Giulio Cesare*. Once known as *Borgo del Pallone* ('Ball Borough') or *Balon* in [Piedmontese dialect](/wiki/Piedmontese_dialect "Piedmontese dialect") (locally [[baˈlun]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")), this neighbourhood is famous for its *mercatino del Balon* or simply *Balon*, the Turinese [flea market](/wiki/Flea_market "Flea market") that opens every Saturday in its tiny and twisted streets. Borgo Dora hosts several remarkable places, such as: *Piccola Casa della Divina Provvidenza* ('Little House of the Divine Providence'), also known as *Cottolengo*, a well-known charitable organisation which has been operating for almost 200 years in the city; *Arsenale della Pace* ('Arsenal of Peace'), a former weapons factory that currently hosts the headquarters of *SERMIG* (*Servizio Missionario Giovani*), a nonprofit association which assists poor and homeless people; *Caserma Cavalli* ('Cavalli Barracks'), one of the most representative buildings of the district, a former barracks topped by a clock tower which now hosts *Scuola Holden*, a storytelling and performing arts school; the evocative *Cortile del Maglio* ('Mallet Courtyard'), a covered pedestrian area featuring bars and clubs. Across from Cortile del Maglio and Arsenale della Pace stands a wide pedestrian area which features a [hot air balloon](/wiki/Hot_air_balloon "Hot air balloon"), a clear allusion to the neighbourhood's old name *Balon*: recently installed, the balloon is open to public which can now take an interesting view of the city from this new high observation point.

Right at the borders of Borgo Dora stands part of *Porta Palazzo* open market which hosts the [New Exhibition Hall](/wiki/New_Exhibition_Hall_(Turin) "New Exhibition Hall (Turin)"), designed by the Italian architect [Massimiliano Fuksas](/wiki/Massimiliano_Fuksas "Massimiliano Fuksas"). The building has replaced the *Clothes Market*, one of the four covered pavilions of Porta Palazzo market, but unfortunately, this glass green-shaded building has been highly criticised because of its lack of usability for commercial activities, albeit an example of contemporary architecture.

Another interesting building at the borders of the neighbourhood is [Porta Milano](/wiki/Torino_Porta_Milano_railway_station "Torino Porta Milano railway station") (a.k.a. *stazione della Ciriè-Lanzo*), a former 19th-century railway station that marked the terminus of Ciriè-Lanzo railway line until the 1980s. To this day, the station is no longer in use as well as the rails up to *Piazza Baldissera*. The station building was recently renovated and now hosts some old locomotives, although it is not open to the public. Unfortunately, the old rails crossing the district are totally disused and neglected, adding decay to the whole area.

Borgo Dora, as many other pockets of Aurora, is characterised by the marked multi-ethnicity of its population, being home to a large community of immigrants from emerging countries.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Santuario_di_Maria_Ausiliatrice_Torino.jpg/250px-Santuario_di_Maria_Ausiliatrice_Torino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santuario_di_Maria_Ausiliatrice_Torino.jpg)

*Santuario di Maria Ausiliatrice*

West of Borgo Dora stands *Rione Valdocco* ('Valdocco neighbourhood'), enclosed by *Via Cigna*, *Corso Regina Margherita*, *Corso Principe Oddone* and the River Dora. This neighbourhood hosts the significant architecture of *Santuario di Maria Ausiliatrice* ('Maria Ausiliatrice Sanctuary') in the homonymous square and behind the church stands *San Pietro in Vincoli* old cemetery.

Overall, the main thoroughfares of the West side of Aurora are *Via Cigna*, which crosses the district from north to south, *Corso Vercelli*, a historical avenue starting north of the River Dora, and *Corso Principe Oddone*, part of the long *Spina Centrale* boulevard that will be built over the underground [Turin-Milan railway](/wiki/Turin-Milan_railway "Turin-Milan railway"). However, the *Spina Centrale* project is proceeding slowly because of the lack of funds and the boulevard is still occupied by a large worksite along its span. Once completed, Aurora district will be connected to Eastern *San Donato*, thanks to a better connection among the roads of the two adjacent districts (i.e. *Corso Ciriè* will continue in *Corso Gamba* and *Strada del Fortino* in *Corso Rosai*).

As for the rest of Aurora, the district is crossed by an important thoroughfare named *Corso Giulio Cesare*, a long boulevard that extends from Porta Palazzo up to [Turin-Trieste motorway](/wiki/Autostrada_A4_(Italy) "Autostrada A4 (Italy)") entrance in the northern urban fringe of Turin. Other significant roads are *Corso Palermo*, *Via Bologna* and *Corso Regio Parco*, mostly in the East side of Aurora which is known as *Borgo Rossini* ('Rossini Borough'). Albeit not a road, the River Dora is also a significant element for the whole district, since it completely crosses it from West to East.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Caff%C3%A8_corretto_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Caff%C3%A8_corretto_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Caff%C3%A8_corretto_(cropped).jpg)

[Lavazza](/wiki/Lavazza "Lavazza"), famous Turin coffee brand

The area north of the river features a mix of old residential buildings and remains of former factories and facilities from the 20th century. An example are the remains of Fiat *Officine Grandi Motori* (*OGM*) in Corso Vigevano, an old factory that produced big industrial and automotive Diesel engines, a sort of symbol of the industrial history of Turin. Another disused facility is *Astanteria Martini* ('Martini Emergency Department') in Via Cigna, a former emergency department from the 1920s which has been lying vacant since long.

As for the old residential buildings of the area, this part of Aurora hosts the oldest [public housing](/wiki/Public_housing "Public housing") block of the city, built by *Istituto Autonomo Case Popolari* (*IACP*) in 1908 in lieu of an old dilapidated small farm once known as *Chiabotto delle Merle*.

Despite its run-down look, the famous [Lavazza](/wiki/Lavazza "Lavazza") coffee company, along with *IAAD* School of Design, chose this part of the city as the location for their new headquarters, which will be built in a contemporary building dubbed *Nuvola* ('Cloud') right at the borders of *Borgo Rossini*. Designed by the architect Gino Zucchi, this project is still a work in progress but excavations in the area revealed the remains of a medieval cemetery and an early Christian basilica; these findings will be preserved and will be shown to the public.

*Borgo Rossini* hosts a number of businesses, for instance, the *[Robe di Kappa](/wiki/Kappa_(company) "Kappa (company)")* flagship store (Kappa is a noted Italian sportswear brand founded in Turin) and the *Cineporto* ('Cineport') a.k.a. *La Casa dei Produttori* ('The Filmmakers' House', which hosts the *Turin Piedmont Film Commission Foundation*).

### Vanchiglia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Vanchiglia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Torino%2C_grattacielo_della_Regione_Piemonte_%2805%29.jpg/250px-Torino%2C_grattacielo_della_Regione_Piemonte_%2805%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torino,_grattacielo_della_Regione_Piemonte_(05).jpg)

[Piedmont Region Headquarters](/wiki/Piedmont_Region_Headquarters "Piedmont Region Headquarters") (209 m; 686 ft), one of the tallest skyscrapers in Italy

*Vanchiglia* is bordered by *Corso San Maurizio*, *Corso Regio Parco* and the [River Po](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)"), crossed also by the [River Dora Riparia](/wiki/Dora_Riparia "Dora Riparia") and by two big avenues, *Corso Regina Margherita* and *Corso Tortona*.

*Borgo Vanchiglia* is the historical district: a little triangle next to downtown, situated between *Corso San Maurizio, Corso Regina Margherita* and the River Po. The district is quite popular nowadays because being quite closer to the heart of Turin nightlife *Piazza Vittorio Veneto*, many bars and restaurants opened recently in this area. However, Vanchiglia also includes the area called *Vanchiglietta*, north of *Borgo Vanchiglia*.

Notable church in *Borgo Vanchiglia* is the French neo-Gothic *Chiesa di Santa Giulia* situated into *Piazza Santa Giulia*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/La_Fetta_di_Polenta_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-La_Fetta_di_Polenta_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Fetta_di_Polenta_-_panoramio.jpg)

*[Fetta Di Polenta](/wiki/Casa_Scaccabarozzi "Casa Scaccabarozzi")*, northern side

A notable and unusual building in the area is the so-called "*[Fetta di Polenta](/wiki/Casa_Scaccabarozzi "Casa Scaccabarozzi")*" (lit. '[polenta](/wiki/Polenta "Polenta") slice'), formerly known as *Casa Scaccabarozzi*. This building is where *Corso San Maurizio* meets *Via Giulia di Barolo*, and it is one of the most peculiar examples of Turin architecture: a thin trapezoid 27 m (89 ft) wide on *Via Giulia Di Barolo*, 5 m (16 ft) on *Corso San Maurizio* and just 0.70 m (2 ft 4 in) wide on the opposite end. It was designed in 1840 by [Alessandro Antonelli](/wiki/Alessandro_Antonelli "Alessandro Antonelli") for his wife, Francesca Scaccabarozzi. The curious name comes from the shape of the palace, which resembles a "slice of polenta", and also because it is painted with an ochre colour.

In the surroundings, in *Via Vanchiglia 8* (although in downtown and not really in *Vanchiglia* anymore), there is another trapezoid house, albeit with less extreme design: similarly, this building is nicknamed "*Fetta di Formaggio*" (lit. 'cheese slice'), built in 1832 for the rich *Marchese Birago di Vische* by the architect *Antonio Talentino*.

Other notable buildings are the town public baths, eclectic building built in 1905 (*Corso Regina Margherita* crossing *Via Vanchiglia*), and the*Teatro della Caduta* theatre, opened in 2003 in *Via Michele Buniva 23*, which with its 45 seats is the smallest theatre in Turin and among the smallest theatres in Europe.

In Corso Regina Margherita, another notable building is the former *Opera pia Reynero*, a charitable organisation. The building was built in 1892. Being abandoned for a long time after it closed in 1996, it was then occupied by the Askatasuna Social Center, a non-profit anarchic organisation, hosting since then various activities such as concerts, dinners, seminars and homeless solidarity initiatives.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Luigi_Einaudi_Campus_-_garden.jpg/250px-Luigi_Einaudi_Campus_-_garden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luigi_Einaudi_Campus_-_garden.jpg)

Campus Luigi Einaudi

North of *Corso Regina Margherita*, district is losing the flavour and architecture typical of Turin downtown, cause a significant portion of the district was formerly occupied by factories, nowadays partially abandoned or replaced by modern buildings. A significant example was the area occupied by gas companies between Corso Regina Margherita and the River Dora, which were partially demolished to make place to the new modern Faculty of Law building (Campus "Luigi Einaudi"), designed by the architect [Norman Foster](/wiki/Norman_Foster_(architect) "Norman Foster (architect)"). This building was classified by the American television company CNN among the 10 most spectacular university buildings in the world.[[43]](#cite_note-45) In the campus courtyard, a large wood statue representing a bull (symbol of Turin) has been erected by [Mario Ceroli](/wiki/Mario_Ceroli "Mario Ceroli"). The area hosts also a student campus.

Next to the campus, a new cycling and pedestrian bridge on the River Dora was opened on 16 April 2010, linking the campus area to *Corso Verona*. *Parco Colletta* is a big park area touched by the two rivers of the district, which also hosts some sport facilities, mainly [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") fields and a swimming pool.

The district is completed by the *[Cimitero Monumentale](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Cimitero_Monumentale_(Torino) "commons:Category:Cimitero Monumentale (Torino)")* cemetery. This huge complex (formerly known as *Cimitero Generale*) is the largest cemetery in Turin, and among the first in Italy for the number of buried people (over 400,000). It is close to the *Colletta* park. The ancient part of the cemetery rises from the main entrance of Corso Novara with his octagonal shape. It contains numerous historical tombs and 12 km (7.5 mi) of arcades, enriched by artistic sculptures (that's why it is called a "monumental cemetery"). Over the years there have been subsequent extensions of the central historical body in the direction of the Colletta park. In the cemetery, there is a crematory temple built in 1882, one of the largest in Italy.

### Main churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Main churches")]

See also: [List of places of worship in Turin](/wiki/List_of_places_of_worship_in_Turin "List of places of worship in Turin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/DuomoTorino.jpg/250px-DuomoTorino.jpg)](/wiki/File:DuomoTorino.jpg)

[Turin Cathedral](/wiki/Turin_Cathedral "Turin Cathedral") featuring the [Chapel of the Holy Shroud](/wiki/Sindone_Chapel "Sindone Chapel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Basilica_di_superga.jpg/250px-Basilica_di_superga.jpg)](/wiki/File:Basilica_di_superga.jpg)

[Basilica of Superga](/wiki/Basilica_of_Superga "Basilica of Superga")

The [Santuario della Consolata](/wiki/Santuario_della_Consolata "Santuario della Consolata"), a sanctuary much frequented by pilgrims, stands on the site of the 10th-century Monastery of St. Andrew, and is a work by Guarini. It was sumptuously restored in 1903. Outside the city are: the [Basilica of Our Lady, Help of Christians](/wiki/Basilica_of_Our_Lady_Help_of_Christians,_Turin "Basilica of Our Lady Help of Christians, Turin") built by St. [John Bosco](/wiki/John_Bosco "John Bosco"), the [Gran Madre](/wiki/Gran_Madre_di_Dio,_Turin "Gran Madre di Dio, Turin") built in 1818 on occasion of the return of King [Victor Emmanuel I of Sardinia](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_I_of_Sardinia "Victor Emmanuel I of Sardinia") and [Santa Maria del Monte](/wiki/Monte_dei_Cappuccini,_Turin "Monte dei Cappuccini, Turin") (1583) on Monte dei Cappuccini.

In the hills overlooking the city, the [Basilica of Superga](/wiki/Basilica_of_Superga "Basilica of Superga") provides a view of Turin against a backdrop of the snow-capped Alps. The basilica holds the tombs of many of the dukes of Savoy, as well as many of the kings of Sardinia. Superga can be reached by means of the [Superga Rack Railway](/wiki/Superga_Rack_Railway "Superga Rack Railway") from Sassi suburb. The Basilica of Superga was built by [Amadeus II of Savoy](/wiki/Amadeus_II_of_Savoy "Amadeus II of Savoy") as an ex-voto for the liberation of Turin (1706), and served as a royal mausoleum since 1772.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

### Villas, parks and gardens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Villas, parks and gardens")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Borgomedievale_sul_PO.jpg/250px-Borgomedievale_sul_PO.jpg)](/wiki/File:Borgomedievale_sul_PO.jpg)

The medieval village in [Valentino Park](/wiki/Parco_del_Valentino "Parco del Valentino")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Area_dei_Laghi.jpg/250px-Area_dei_Laghi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Area_dei_Laghi.jpg)

[La Mandria Regional Park](/wiki/La_Mandria_Regional_Park "La Mandria Regional Park")

The most popular park in the city is [Parco del Valentino](/wiki/Parco_del_Valentino "Parco del Valentino"). In 1961, during the celebrations of *Italia61* ([Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") centenary), an important international exhibition (*FLOR61: Flowers of the world in Turin*) took place in the park with 800 exhibitors from 19 countries. For the occasion the plan for the new lighting of the park, along with its fountains and paths, was assigned to Guido Chiarelli, the head engineer at the city hall.

Other large parks are *Parco della Pellerina*, *Parco Colletta*, *Parco Rignon*, *Parco Colonnetti* and the [University botanical gardens](/wiki/Orto_Botanico_dell%27Universit%C3%A0_di_Torino "Orto Botanico dell'Università di Torino"). Around the city are several other parks such as [La Mandria Regional Park](/wiki/La_Mandria_Regional_Park "La Mandria Regional Park") and the [Parco della Palazzina di Caccia di Stupinigi](/wiki/Parco_naturale_di_Stupinigi "Parco naturale di Stupinigi"), once hunting grounds of the Savoy, and those on the hills of Turin. Many parks are smaller, in the various districts: there is also a total of 240 playgrounds in these parks. In the early 1960s, mayor [Amedeo Peyron](/w/index.php?title=Amedeo_Peyron&action=edit&redlink=1 "Amedeo Peyron (page does not exist)") had the first garden in Italy with games for children inaugurated. According to a [Legambiente](/wiki/Legambiente "Legambiente") report from 2007, Turin is the first Italian city as far as structures and policies on childcare are concerned.[[45]](#cite_note-47) One of the most famous parks featuring a children's playground is *Parco della Tesoriera*, which is also home to *Andrea della Corte Municipal Music Library*; this facility is housed in [Villa Tesoriera](/wiki/Villa_Tesoriera "Villa Tesoriera"), built in 1715 and once the Royal Treasurer's residence. The park is in the Parella suburb (Turin's West Side) and hosts many concerts in summer.

[Rosa Vercellana](/wiki/Rosa_Vercellana "Rosa Vercellana"), commonly known as *Rosina* and, in [Piedmontese](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language") as *La Bela Rosin* ('The Beautiful Rosin'), was the [mistress](/wiki/Mistress_(lover) "Mistress (lover)") and later wife of King [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel II of Italy"). She was made Countess of Mirafiori and Fontanafredda, but never Queen of Italy. As the Savoy family refused to allow her to be buried next to her husband in the [Pantheon](/wiki/Pantheon,_Rome "Pantheon, Rome"), her children had a mausoleum built for her in a similar form and on a smaller scale in Turin, next to the road to the Castello di Mirafiori. The circular copper-domed [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") monument, surmounted by a [Latin cross](/wiki/Latin_cross "Latin cross") and surrounded by a large park, was designed by [Angelo Dimezzi](/w/index.php?title=Angelo_Dimezzi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Angelo Dimezzi (page does not exist)") and completed in 1888.[[46]](#cite_note-48)[[47]](#cite_note-parks-49)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Turin](/wiki/Economy_of_Turin "Economy of Turin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/LingottoPalazzina4.jpg/250px-LingottoPalazzina4.jpg)](/wiki/File:LingottoPalazzina4.jpg)

The [Lingotto](/wiki/Lingotto "Lingotto") building in Via Nizza was once the world headquarters of [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat")
