Turin developed as a [Fordist](/wiki/Fordism "Fordism") city in the early 20th century, which meant a shift from a service-based economy to an industry-based one.[[48]](#cite_note-50) In the vein of many Fordist economies Turin's economy relies heavily upon its automotive and aerospace industries.[[49]](#cite_note-:0-51)[[50]](#cite_note-52) Despite the general decline of the automotive industry since the [oil crisis of 1973](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis"), the city still relies heavily upon its automotive industry. Since before the Second World War, the automotive industry has been the largest employer in the city, and almost all exports from Turin are manufactured goods.[[49]](#cite_note-:0-51)[[51]](#cite_note-53) The city serves as the headquarters to [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat") (*Fabbrica Italiana Automobili Torino*; Turin Italian Automobiles Factory), which has since been absorbed by its parent company, the [Fiat Chrysler Automobiles](/wiki/Fiat_Chrysler_Automobiles "Fiat Chrysler Automobiles") group (now [Stellantis](/wiki/Stellantis "Stellantis")) headquartered in Amsterdam, the eighth largest automotive company in the world.[[52]](#cite_note-54) Turin is still home to a sizeable Fiat factory.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

From the 1980s, Turin diversified its economy and is shifting back towards a service economy. Tech and innovation industries are booming in Turin, which was ranked third in number of innovative startups and firms in the information-tech sector, and has some of the most patent applications to the [European Patent Office](/wiki/European_Patent_Office "European Patent Office") of any city.[[49]](#cite_note-:0-51)[[54]](#cite_note-56) In 2008 the city generated a [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") of $68 billion, ranking as the world's 78th richest city by purchasing power,[[55]](#cite_note-57) and 16th in Europe, according to [PricewaterhouseCoopers](/wiki/PricewaterhouseCoopers "PricewaterhouseCoopers").[[56]](#cite_note-58) Turin accounts for 8 percent of Italy's GDP.[[57]](#cite_note-59) The city has been ranked in 2010 by [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") as a [Gamma-level city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city").

Other companies operating in Turin are [Maserati](/wiki/Maserati "Maserati"), [Lancia](/wiki/Lancia "Lancia"), [Alfa Romeo](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo "Alfa Romeo"), [Iveco](/wiki/Iveco "Iveco"), [Pininfarina](/wiki/Pininfarina "Pininfarina"), [Bertone](/wiki/Gruppo_Bertone "Gruppo Bertone"), [Sparco](/wiki/Sparco "Sparco"), [Italdesign Giugiaro](/wiki/Italdesign_Giugiaro "Italdesign Giugiaro"), [New Holland](/wiki/New_Holland_Agriculture "New Holland Agriculture"), [Comau](/wiki/Comau "Comau"), [Magneti Marelli](/wiki/Magneti_Marelli "Magneti Marelli"), [Graziano Oerlikon](/wiki/Graziano_Trasmissioni "Graziano Trasmissioni"), [Ghia](/wiki/Carrozzeria_Ghia "Carrozzeria Ghia"), [Fioravanti](/wiki/Fioravanti_(automotive) "Fioravanti (automotive)") (automotive), [Rai](/wiki/RAI "RAI") (national broadcasting company), [Banca Investis](/wiki/Banca_Intermobiliare "Banca Intermobiliare"), [FCA Bank](/wiki/FCA_Bank "FCA Bank"), [Intesa Sanpaolo](/wiki/Intesa_Sanpaolo "Intesa Sanpaolo"), Reale Mutua (finance), [Invicta](/wiki/Invicta_(company) "Invicta (company)"), [Kappa](/wiki/Kappa_(company) "Kappa (company)"), [Superga](/wiki/Superga_(brand) "Superga (brand)") (fashion), [Ferrero](/wiki/Ferrero_SpA "Ferrero SpA"), [Lavazza](/wiki/Lavazza "Lavazza"), [Martini & Rossi](/wiki/Martini_%26_Rossi "Martini & Rossi") (food & beverage), [Alpitour](/w/index.php?title=Alpitour&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alpitour (page does not exist)") (hospitality and tourism), [TILab](/w/index.php?title=TILab&action=edit&redlink=1 "TILab (page does not exist)") (ex-[CSELT](/wiki/CSELT "CSELT")), and [Aurora (pen manufacturer)](/wiki/Aurora_(pen_manufacturer) "Aurora (pen manufacturer)").

The city is also well known for its [aerospace](/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace") industry [Alenia Aeronautica](/wiki/Alenia_Aeronautica "Alenia Aeronautica"), [Thales Alenia Space](/wiki/Thales_Alenia_Space "Thales Alenia Space") and [Avio](/wiki/Avio "Avio"). The [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station") modules [Harmony](/wiki/Harmony_(ISS_module) "Harmony (ISS module)"), [Columbus](/wiki/Columbus_(ISS_module) "Columbus (ISS module)"), [Tranquility](/wiki/Tranquility_(ISS_module) "Tranquility (ISS module)"), as well as the [Cupola](/wiki/Cupola_(ISS_module) "Cupola (ISS module)") and all [MPLMs](/wiki/Multi-Purpose_Logistics_Module "Multi-Purpose Logistics Module") were produced in Turin. The future European launcher projects beyond [Ariane 5](/wiki/Ariane_5 "Ariane 5") will also be managed from Turin by the new [NGL](/wiki/NGL_Prime_SpA "NGL Prime SpA") company, a subsidiary of [EADS](/wiki/EADS "EADS") (70%) and Aircraft Division of [Leonardo](/wiki/Leonardo_S.p.A. "Leonardo S.p.A.") (30%).

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Visual art and museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Visual art and museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Nichelino_Palazzina_di_Caccia_di_Stupinigi_Esterno_17.jpg/250px-Nichelino_Palazzina_di_Caccia_di_Stupinigi_Esterno_17.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nichelino_Palazzina_di_Caccia_di_Stupinigi_Esterno_17.jpg)

The royal hunting lodge of [Stupinigi](/wiki/Palazzina_di_caccia_of_Stupinigi "Palazzina di caccia of Stupinigi")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Museo_Egizio_di_Torino-631_o.jpg/250px-Museo_Egizio_di_Torino-631_o.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museo_Egizio_di_Torino-631_o.jpg)

The *[Museo Egizio](/wiki/Museo_Egizio "Museo Egizio")* is the world's oldest Egyptian museum.

Turin, as the former capital of the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)") and the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"), is home of the [Savoy Residences](/wiki/Residences_of_the_Royal_House_of_Savoy "Residences of the Royal House of Savoy"). In addition to the 17th-century [Royal Palace](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Turin "Royal Palace of Turin"), built for Madama Reale [Christine Marie of France](/wiki/Christine_Marie_of_France "Christine Marie of France") (the official residence of the Savoys until 1865) there are many palaces, residences and castles in the city centre and in the surrounding towns. Turin is home to [Palazzo Chiablese](/wiki/Palazzo_Chiablese "Palazzo Chiablese"), the [Royal Armoury](/wiki/Royal_Armoury_of_Turin "Royal Armoury of Turin"), the [Royal Library](/wiki/Royal_Library_of_Turin "Royal Library of Turin"), [Palazzo Madama](/wiki/Palazzo_Madama,_Turin "Palazzo Madama, Turin"), [Palazzo Carignano](/wiki/Palazzo_Carignano "Palazzo Carignano"), [Villa della Regina](/wiki/Villa_della_Regina "Villa della Regina"), and the [Valentino Castle](/wiki/Castello_del_Valentino "Castello del Valentino"). The complex of the [Residences of the Royal House of Savoy](/wiki/Residences_of_the_Royal_House_of_Savoy "Residences of the Royal House of Savoy") in Turin and in the nearby cities of [Rivoli](/wiki/Rivoli,_Piedmont "Rivoli, Piedmont"), [Moncalieri](/wiki/Moncalieri "Moncalieri"), [Venaria Reale](/wiki/Venaria_Reale "Venaria Reale"), [Agliè](/wiki/Agli%C3%A8 "Agliè"), [Racconigi](/wiki/Racconigi "Racconigi"), [Stupinigi](/wiki/Stupinigi "Stupinigi"), [Pollenzo](/wiki/Pollentia "Pollentia") and [Govone](/wiki/Govone "Govone") was declared a [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") in 1997. UNESCO also recognises Turin as a "[Design City](/wiki/Design_Cities_(UNESCO) "Design Cities (UNESCO)")." In recent years, Turin has become an increasingly popular tourist destination, ranking 203rd in the world and tenth in Italy in 2008, with about 240,000 tourist arrivals.[[14]](#cite_note-euromonitor.com-16)

The [Egyptian Museum of Turin](/wiki/Museo_Egizio "Museo Egizio") specialises in archaeology and anthropology, in particular the [art of ancient Egypt](/wiki/Art_of_ancient_Egypt "Art of ancient Egypt"). It is home to what is regarded as one of the largest collections of Egyptian antiquities outside of Egypt. In 2006 it received more than 500,000 visitors.[[58]](#cite_note-60) The [Museum of Oriental Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Oriental_Art_(Turin) "Museum of Oriental Art (Turin)") houses one of the most important [Asian art](/wiki/Asian_art "Asian art") collections in Italy.[[59]](#cite_note-rep-mao-01-61)[[60]](#cite_note-rep-mao-02-62)

Other museums include the [National Museum of Cinema](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Cinema "National Museum of Cinema"), the [Museo Nazionale dell'Automobile](/wiki/Museo_Nazionale_dell%27Automobile "Museo Nazionale dell'Automobile"), the [J-Museum](/wiki/J-Museum "J-Museum"), the [Museum of Human Anatomy Luigi Rolando](/wiki/Museum_of_Human_Anatomy_Luigi_Rolando "Museum of Human Anatomy Luigi Rolando"), the Museo delle Marionette (puppet museum) and the Museo Nazionale della Montagna (National Museum of the Mountains). Art museums include the [Sabauda Gallery](/wiki/Sabauda_Gallery "Sabauda Gallery"), the [Museo Civico d'Arte Antica](/wiki/Museo_Civico_d%27Arte_Antica "Museo Civico d'Arte Antica"), [Pinacoteca Giovanni e Marella Agnelli](/wiki/Pinacoteca_Giovanni_e_Marella_Agnelli "Pinacoteca Giovanni e Marella Agnelli"), the [Accademia Albertina](/wiki/Accademia_Albertina "Accademia Albertina"), the [Gallery of Modern and Contemporary Art](/wiki/Turin_Civic_Gallery_of_Modern_and_Contemporary_Art "Turin Civic Gallery of Modern and Contemporary Art"), and the [Diocesan Museum of Turin](/wiki/Diocesan_Museum_of_Turin "Diocesan Museum of Turin").

After it had been little more than a town for a long time, in 1559 the Duke [Emmanuel Philibert](/wiki/Emmanuel_Philibert,_Duke_of_Savoy "Emmanuel Philibert, Duke of Savoy") of Savoy made Turin the capital of his domains. The Duke had the ambition to transform the city into a major artistic and cultural capital, and in the following centuries numerous artists were to work at the Savoy court, especially architects and planners such as [Carlo di Castellamonte](/wiki/Carlo_di_Castellamonte "Carlo di Castellamonte") and his son [Amedeo](/wiki/Amedeo_di_Castellamonte "Amedeo di Castellamonte"), [Guarino Guarini](/wiki/Guarino_Guarini "Guarino Guarini") and, in the 18th century, [Filippo Juvarra](/wiki/Filippo_Juvarra "Filippo Juvarra") and [Benedetto Alfieri](/wiki/Benedetto_Alfieri "Benedetto Alfieri").

As for the painting and the visual arts, Turin became a point of reference, especially in the 20th century. In the 1920s, the painter [Felice Casorati](/wiki/Felice_Casorati_(painter) "Felice Casorati (painter)") inspired a number of students called [The group of six of Turin](/wiki/The_group_of_six_of_Turin "The group of six of Turin") and these included [Carlo Levi](/wiki/Carlo_Levi "Carlo Levi"), [Henry Paolucci](/wiki/Henry_Paolucci "Henry Paolucci"), [Gigi Chessa](/wiki/Gigi_Chessa "Gigi Chessa"), [Francesco Menzio](/wiki/Francesco_Menzio "Francesco Menzio"), [Nicola Galante](/w/index.php?title=Nicola_Galante&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nicola Galante (page does not exist)") and [Jessie Boswell](/wiki/Jessie_Boswell "Jessie Boswell"). Artists born in Turin include the sculptor [Umberto Mastroianni](/wiki/Umberto_Mastroianni "Umberto Mastroianni") and the architect [Carlo Mollino](/wiki/Carlo_Mollino "Carlo Mollino"). Between the 1960s and the 1970s, the international centre of Turin ([Arte Povera](/wiki/Arte_Povera "Arte Povera")), the presence in the city of artists such as [Alighiero Boetti](/wiki/Alighiero_Boetti "Alighiero Boetti"), [Mario Merz](/wiki/Mario_Merz "Mario Merz"), [Giuseppe Penone](/wiki/Giuseppe_Penone "Giuseppe Penone"), [Piero Gilardi](/wiki/Piero_Gilardi "Piero Gilardi") and [Michelangelo Pistoletto](/wiki/Michelangelo_Pistoletto "Michelangelo Pistoletto"). In those years there was a strong artistic influence of designer [Armando Testa](/wiki/Armando_Testa "Armando Testa"). Artists currently operating in the city include [Ugo Nespolo](/wiki/Ugo_Nespolo "Ugo Nespolo") and [Carol Rama](/wiki/Carol_Rama "Carol Rama").

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Teatro_Regio_%28Turin%29.jpg/250px-Teatro_Regio_%28Turin%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Teatro_Regio_(Turin).jpg)

Teatro Regio opera house

The city's main opera house is [Teatro Regio di Torino](/wiki/Teatro_Regio_di_Torino "Teatro Regio di Torino"), where Puccini premiered his *[La bohème](/wiki/La_boh%C3%A8me "La bohème")* in 1896.[[61]](#cite_note-Budden-63) It was burned down in 1936 and was rebuilt after World War II. On 8 October 2021, the [European Broadcasting Union](/wiki/European_Broadcasting_Union "European Broadcasting Union") (EBU) and [RAI](/wiki/RAI "RAI") announced that the city would host the [Eurovision Song Contest 2022](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_2022 "Eurovision Song Contest 2022"), following Italy's victory at the [2021](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_2021 "Eurovision Song Contest 2021") contest in [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), with the song "[Zitti e buoni](/wiki/Zitti_e_buoni "Zitti e buoni")", performed by [Måneskin](/wiki/M%C3%A5neskin "Måneskin"). The contest took place at the [Pala Alpitour](/wiki/Pala_Alpitour "Pala Alpitour"), with both semi-finals of the contest took place on 10 and 12 May, and the grand final on 14 May. It was the first time that Turin has hosted the contest and the third time that Italy has hosted the contest overall, with the last being in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") in [1991](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1991 "Eurovision Song Contest 1991").[[62]](#cite_note-64)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Torino%2C_biblioteca_Nazionale_%2801%29.jpg/250px-Torino%2C_biblioteca_Nazionale_%2801%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torino,_biblioteca_Nazionale_(01).jpg)

National Library

A literary centre for many centuries, Turin began to attract writers only after the establishment of the court of the [Dukes of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"). One of the most famous writers of the 17th century was [Giambattista Marino](/wiki/Giambattista_Marino "Giambattista Marino"), which in 1608 moved to the court of [Charles Emmanuel I](/wiki/Charles_Emmanuel_I,_Duke_of_Savoy "Charles Emmanuel I, Duke of Savoy"). Marino suffered an assassination attempt by a rival, [Gaspare Murtola](/wiki/Gaspare_Murtola "Gaspare Murtola"), and was later imprisoned for a year because of gossip that he had said and written against the duke. Perhaps, because of this, in 1615 Marino left Turin and moved to France.

The main literary figures during the Baroque age in Turin were [Emanuele Tesauro](/wiki/Emanuele_Tesauro "Emanuele Tesauro") and [Alessandro Tassoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Tassoni "Alessandro Tassoni"). In the next century Torino hosted the poet [Vittorio Alfieri](/wiki/Vittorio_Alfieri "Vittorio Alfieri") from Asti for a while. The situation was very different in the 19th century, especially since the city became a point of reference for Italian unification and, subsequently, the capital of the Kingdom of Italy. Indeed, in those years Tommaseo, Settembrini and [John Meadows](/w/index.php?title=John_Meadows_(author)&action=edit&redlink=1 "John Meadows (author) (page does not exist)") resided in the city. A major literary and cultural woman of that time was [Olimpia Savio](/wiki/Olimpia_Savio "Olimpia Savio"). In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, Turin was home to writers such as [Guido Gozzano](/wiki/Guido_Gozzano "Guido Gozzano"), [Edmondo De Amicis](/wiki/Edmondo_De_Amicis "Edmondo De Amicis"), [Emilio Salgari](/wiki/Emilio_Salgari "Emilio Salgari") and Dino Segre, the latter known by the pseudonym of [Pitigrilli](/wiki/Pitigrilli "Pitigrilli").

Turin had a very important role in Italian literature after World War II. A major publishing house, [Giulio Einaudi](/wiki/Giulio_Einaudi "Giulio Einaudi"), published works by authors such as [Cesare Pavese](/wiki/Cesare_Pavese "Cesare Pavese"), [Italo Calvino](/wiki/Italo_Calvino "Italo Calvino"), [Vitaliano Brancati](/wiki/Vitaliano_Brancati "Vitaliano Brancati"), [Primo Levi](/wiki/Primo_Levi "Primo Levi"), [Natalia Ginzburg](/wiki/Natalia_Ginzburg "Natalia Ginzburg"), [Fernanda Pivano](/wiki/Fernanda_Pivano "Fernanda Pivano"), [Beppe Fenoglio](/wiki/Beppe_Fenoglio "Beppe Fenoglio"), [Carlo Fruttero](/wiki/Carlo_Fruttero "Carlo Fruttero") and [Franco Lucentini](/wiki/Franco_Lucentini "Franco Lucentini"). In more recent years, writers active in the city are [Giovanni Arpino](/wiki/Giovanni_Arpino "Giovanni Arpino"), [Nico Orengo](/w/index.php?title=Nico_Orengo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nico Orengo (page does not exist)"), [Giuseppe Culicchia](/w/index.php?title=Giuseppe_Culicchia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Giuseppe Culicchia (page does not exist)"), [Margherita Oggero](/wiki/Margherita_Oggero "Margherita Oggero"), [Laura Mancinelli](/wiki/Laura_Mancinelli "Laura Mancinelli"), [Alessandra Montrucchio](/w/index.php?title=Alessandra_Montrucchio&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alessandra Montrucchio (page does not exist)"), [Alessandro Perissinotto](/wiki/Alessandro_Perissinotto "Alessandro Perissinotto"), [Guido Quartz](/w/index.php?title=Guido_Quartz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Guido Quartz (page does not exist)"), [Piero Soria](/w/index.php?title=Piero_Soria&action=edit&redlink=1 "Piero Soria (page does not exist)") and [Alessandro Baricco](/wiki/Alessandro_Baricco "Alessandro Baricco"). Baricco was also among the founders of the [Scuola Holden](/w/index.php?title=Scuola_Holden&action=edit&redlink=1 "Scuola Holden (page does not exist)"), dedicated to writing techniques teaching.

There is also a literary tradition in the local [Piedmontese language](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language"), including authors such as [Nicoletto da Torino](/wiki/Nicoletto_da_Torino "Nicoletto da Torino"), [Ignas Isler](/w/index.php?title=Ignas_Isler&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ignas Isler (page does not exist)") (author of epic poems), and [Eduard Calv](/w/index.php?title=Eduard_Calv&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eduard Calv (page does not exist)").

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Religion")]

The city is home to the well-known [Shroud of Turin](/wiki/Shroud_of_Turin "Shroud of Turin"): a linen cloth bearing the image of a man who appears to have suffered physical trauma in a manner consistent with crucifixion. The shroud is taken by many Christians to be a miraculous image of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") at the time of his death. It is kept in the royal chapel of the Cathedral of Saint John the Baptist in the city centre. The shroud is one of the city's main symbols and tourist attractions, it is a symbol of [religious devotion](/wiki/Catholic_devotion "Catholic devotion").

### Science and technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Science and technology")]

Turin had an astronomical observatory used by [Giovanni Plana](/wiki/Giovanni_Plana "Giovanni Plana"). The scientist [Amedeo Avogadro](/wiki/Amedeo_Avogadro "Amedeo Avogadro") worked as a professor in Turin. [Galileo Ferraris](/wiki/Galileo_Ferraris "Galileo Ferraris"), a professor at Turin University, discovered the working principles of the [electric motor](/wiki/Electric_motor "Electric motor") during the 19th century.

In modern times, Turin hosted the [CSELT](/wiki/CSELT "CSELT") telco laboratory.

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [List of radio stations in Turin](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_Turin "List of radio stations in Turin")

After [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") and [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), Turin was chosen by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as [World Book Capital](/wiki/World_Book_Capital "World Book Capital") for the year 2006. The [International Book Fair](/wiki/Turin_International_Book_Fair "Turin International Book Fair") is one of the most important fairs of its type in Europe. Turin is home to one of Italy's principal national newspapers, *[La Stampa](/wiki/La_Stampa "La Stampa")*, and the sports daily newspaper *[Tuttosport](/wiki/Tuttosport "Tuttosport")*. The city is also served by other publications such as the Turin editions of *[La Repubblica](/wiki/La_Repubblica "La Repubblica")*, *[il Giornale](/wiki/Il_Giornale "Il Giornale")*, *[Leggo](/wiki/Leggo "Leggo")*, *[City](/wiki/City_(newspaper) "City (newspaper)")*, *[Metro](/wiki/Metro_(Italy) "Metro (Italy)")* and *[E Polis](/wiki/E_Polis_Torino "E Polis Torino")*. [RAI](/wiki/RAI "RAI") has had a production centre in Turin since 1954.

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sport")]

Main article: [Sports in Turin](/wiki/Sports_in_Turin "Sports in Turin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Juventus_v_Real_Madrid%2C_Champions_League%2C_Stadium%2C_Turin%2C_2013.jpg/250px-Juventus_v_Real_Madrid%2C_Champions_League%2C_Stadium%2C_Turin%2C_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Juventus_v_Real_Madrid,_Champions_League,_Stadium,_Turin,_2013.jpg)

The [Juventus Stadium](/wiki/Juventus_Stadium "Juventus Stadium"), home of [Juventus FC](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Stadio_Olimpico_di_Torino.jpg/250px-Stadio_Olimpico_di_Torino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stadio_Olimpico_di_Torino.jpg)

The [Stadio Olimpico Grande Torino](/wiki/Stadio_Olimpico_Grande_Torino "Stadio Olimpico Grande Torino"), home of [Torino FC](/wiki/Torino_FC "Torino FC")

The city has a rich sporting heritage as the home to two historically significant football teams: [Juventus FC](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") (founded in 1897) and [Torino FC](/wiki/Torino_FC "Torino FC") (founded in 1906). Juventus has the larger fan base, especially all over Italy and worldwide, while Torino enjoys a greater support in the city itself. The two clubs contest the oldest [derby](/wiki/Local_derby "Local derby") in Italy, the [Derby della Mole](/wiki/Derby_della_Mole "Derby della Mole").[[63]](#cite_note-65)

Juventus is [Italy](/wiki/Football_in_Italy "Football in Italy")'s [most successful football club](/wiki/Football_records_in_Italy#Overall "Football records in Italy") and one of the most successful in the world.[[64]](#cite_note-66) It ranks joint twelfth in the list of the world's clubs with the most official international titles (sixth between European clubs),[[65]](#cite_note-UEFA_club_competitions-67) and was [the first](/wiki/Timeline_of_football_(soccer)#1980s "Timeline of football (soccer)") in association football history—remaining the only one in the world (as of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit), after the first [UEFA Europa Conference League Final](/wiki/2022_UEFA_Europa_Conference_League_Final "2022 UEFA Europa Conference League Final"))—to have won all possible official [continental competitions](/wiki/European_football "European football") and the [world title](/wiki/List_of_world_champion_football_clubs "List of world champion football clubs").[[66]](#cite_note-official-68) Juventus' owned ground, the [Juventus Stadium](/wiki/Juventus_Stadium "Juventus Stadium"), was inaugurated in 2011 and hosted the [2014 UEFA Europa League final](/wiki/2014_UEFA_Europa_League_final "2014 UEFA Europa League final"), the [2021 Nations League finals](/wiki/2021_UEFA_Nations_League_Finals "2021 UEFA Nations League Finals") and the [2022 Women's Champions League final](/wiki/2022_UEFA_Women%27s_Champions_League_final "2022 UEFA Women's Champions League final"). This was the first time the city hosted a seasonal UEFA club competition's single-match final. Juventus' [female team](/wiki/Juventus_F.C._(women) "Juventus F.C. (women)") has won, among others, five straight [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A_(women) "Serie A (women)") titles since its inception in 2017, becoming one of the most winning teams in the country.

Torino FC was founded by the union of one of the oldest football teams in Turin, [Football Club Torinese](/wiki/Football_Club_Torinese "Football Club Torinese") (founded in 1894), with breakaways from Juventus and was the most successful team, called "[Grande Torino](/wiki/Grande_Torino "Grande Torino")", in the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") during the 1940s. In 1949, in the [Superga air disaster](/wiki/Superga_air_disaster "Superga air disaster"), a plane carrying almost the whole team crashed into the [Basilica of Superga](/wiki/Basilica_of_Superga "Basilica of Superga") in the Turin hills. Torino currently plays its home games at the [Stadio Olimpico "Grande Torino"](/wiki/Stadio_Olimpico_di_Torino "Stadio Olimpico di Torino"), named after the team of the 1940s, which was a host stadium for the [1934 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1934_FIFA_World_Cup "1934 FIFA World Cup") and the venue of the opening ceremonies of the [XX Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics") and [IX Paralympic Winter Games](/wiki/IX_Paralympic_Winter_Games "IX Paralympic Winter Games") and the closing ceremony of the former;[[67]](#cite_note-69) moreover the team recently rebuilt the historic [Stadio Filadelfia](/wiki/Stadio_Filadelfia "Stadio Filadelfia"), used for games of the youth teams and trainings of the first squad, and the planned seat of the team museum.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

The city hosted the final stages of the [EuroBasket 1979](/wiki/EuroBasket_1979 "EuroBasket 1979"). The most important [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") club team is the [Auxilium Torino](/wiki/Auxilium_Pallacanestro_Torino "Auxilium Pallacanestro Torino"), refounded in 2009, playing in the Italian [LBA](/wiki/Lega_Basket_Serie_A "Lega Basket Serie A"). In 2018 Auxilium Torino went to win its first [Italian Basketball Cup](/wiki/Italian_Basketball_Cup "Italian Basketball Cup") ever.[[69]](#cite_note-71) Turin hosted the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics") in February 2006. Turin is the largest city to have ever hosted a Winter Olympics, and was the largest metropolitan area to host them at the time.[[70]](#cite_note-Turin_metro-72)[[71]](#cite_note-Turin_largest-73)[[72]](#cite_note-slc_explanation-74) The city was awarded with the title of European Capital of Sport 2015.[[73]](#cite_note-75) The candidature sees the city strongly committed to increasing sports activities.[[74]](#cite_note-76)[[75]](#cite_note-77) The city hosted the [ATP Finals](/wiki/ATP_Finals "ATP Finals") tennis event from 2021 to 2025.[[76]](#cite_note-78) Turin hosted the [2025 Special Olympics World Winter Games](/wiki/2025_Special_Olympics_World_Winter_Games "2025 Special Olympics World Winter Games").[[77]](#cite_note-79)

### Cinema

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Cinema")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Mole_Antonelliana_02.jpg/250px-Mole_Antonelliana_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mole_Antonelliana_02.jpg)

[Mole Antonelliana](/wiki/Mole_Antonelliana "Mole Antonelliana")

Turin is the Italian city where film [chromatography](/wiki/History_of_chromatography "History of chromatography") was first established. As such, it forms the birthplace of [Italian cinema](/wiki/Italian_cinema "Italian cinema"). Because of its historic, geographical and cultural proximity to France, Italian filmmakers were naturally influenced by [French cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_France "Cinema of France") and the [Lumière brothers](/wiki/Auguste_and_Louis_Lumi%C3%A8re "Auguste and Louis Lumière"). The first Italian cinema screening occurred in Turin in March 1896. In November 1896, Italian filmmakers performed the first cinema screening of a film before a fee-paying audience.[[78]](#cite_note-80)

By the start of the 20th century (especially after 1907), a number of the first Italian films were aired in Turin. Examples include [Giovanni Pastrone](/wiki/Giovanni_Pastrone "Giovanni Pastrone") *[Cabiria](/wiki/Cabiria "Cabiria")*, in 1914, one of the first [blockbusters](/wiki/Blockbuster_(entertainment) "Blockbuster (entertainment)") in history.

The Turin-based company [Ambrosio Film](/wiki/Ambrosio_Film "Ambrosio Film"), established in 1906 by [Arturo Ambrosio](/wiki/Arturo_Ambrosio "Arturo Ambrosio"), was one of the leading forces in Italian cinema and boosted the importance of the city as a filmmaking destination. The company, noted in particular for its historical epics, produced a large number of films until it was dissolved in 1924.

During the 1920s and 30s, Turin hosted a number of film productions and major film studios (*film houses*), such as the [Itala film](/w/index.php?title=Itala_film&action=edit&redlink=1 "Itala film (page does not exist)"), [Aquila](/wiki/Aquila_(studio) "Aquila (studio)") and [Fert Studios](/w/index.php?title=Fert_Studios&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fert Studios (page does not exist)"). Today their heritage is in the modern [Lumiq Studios](/wiki/Lumiq_Studios "Lumiq Studios")[[79]](#cite_note-81) and [Virtual Reality Multi Media Spa](/w/index.php?title=Virtual_Reality_Multi_Media_Spa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Virtual Reality Multi Media Spa (page does not exist)").[[80]](#cite_note-82)[[81]](#cite_note-83) Turin's prominence in Italian film continued until 1937, the year [Cinecittà](/wiki/Cinecitt%C3%A0 "Cinecittà") was inaugurated in Rome.

After World War II, the cinematic scene in Turin continued to thrive. 1956 saw the opening of the [National Museum of Cinema](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Cinema "National Museum of Cinema"), first housed in the [Palazzo Chiablese](/wiki/Palazzo_Chiablese "Palazzo Chiablese") and then, from 2000, in the imposing headquarters of the [Mole Antonelliana](/wiki/Mole_Antonelliana "Mole Antonelliana"). In 1982 the film critic [Gianni Rondolino](/wiki/Gianni_Rondolino "Gianni Rondolino") created Festival Internazionale Cinema Giovani,[[82]](#cite_note-84) which later became the [Torino Film Festival](/wiki/Torino_Film_Festival "Torino Film Festival").

Today Turin is one of the main cinematographic and television centres in Italy, thanks to the role of the [Turin Film Commission](/w/index.php?title=Turin_Film_Commission&action=edit&redlink=1 "Turin Film Commission (page does not exist)") that reports the production of many feature films, soap operas and commercials.

Turin streets were the locations where [Audrey Hepburn](/wiki/Audrey_Hepburn "Audrey Hepburn") played *[War and Peace](/wiki/War_and_Peace_(1956_film) "War and Peace (1956 film)")*, [Michael Caine](/wiki/Michael_Caine "Michael Caine") drove a Mini Cooper in *[The Italian Job](/wiki/The_Italian_Job "The Italian Job")*, [Claudio Bisio](/wiki/Claudio_Bisio "Claudio Bisio") becomes the president of the Italian Republic, [Carlo Verdone](/wiki/Carlo_Verdone "Carlo Verdone") set his version of *[Cinderella](/w/index.php?title=Cinderella_(2013_film)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cinderella (2013 film) (page does not exist)")*, [Marco Tullio Giordana](/wiki/Marco_Tullio_Giordana "Marco Tullio Giordana") shot *[Piazza Fontana: The Italian Conspiracy](/wiki/Piazza_Fontana:_The_Italian_Conspiracy "Piazza Fontana: The Italian Conspiracy")*, [Woody Allen](/wiki/Woody_Allen "Woody Allen") shot *[Hannah and Her Sisters](/wiki/Hannah_and_Her_Sisters "Hannah and Her Sisters")*, [Cate Blanchett](/wiki/Cate_Blanchett "Cate Blanchett") played *[Heaven](/wiki/Heaven_(2002_film) "Heaven (2002 film)")*, [Giovanna Mezzogiorno](/wiki/Giovanna_Mezzogiorno "Giovanna Mezzogiorno") *[Vincere](/wiki/Vincere "Vincere")*, [Marcello Mastroianni](/wiki/Marcello_Mastroianni "Marcello Mastroianni") and [Jacqueline Bisset](/wiki/Jacqueline_Bisset "Jacqueline Bisset") *[The Sunday Woman](/wiki/The_Sunday_Woman_(film) "The Sunday Woman (film)")*, and [Harvey Keitel](/wiki/Harvey_Keitel "Harvey Keitel") *[The Stone Merchant](/wiki/The_Stone_Merchant "The Stone Merchant")*. Turin also became the capital of the tsar for *[The Demons of St. Petersberg](/wiki/The_Demons_of_St._Petersberg "The Demons of St. Petersberg")*.

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Gianduiotti.jpg/250px-Gianduiotti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gianduiotti.jpg)

The iconic *[gianduiotto](/wiki/Gianduiotto "Gianduiotto")* chocolate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/2016_1128_Turin_Bicerin_02.jpg/250px-2016_1128_Turin_Bicerin_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:2016_1128_Turin_Bicerin_02.jpg)

*[Bicerin](/wiki/Bicerin "Bicerin")* chocolate drink served in its trademark rounded glass

Turin is well known for its chocolate production, especially for its traditional, ingot-shaped chocolate called *[gianduiotto](/wiki/Gianduiotto "Gianduiotto")*, named after [Gianduja](/wiki/Gianduja_(commedia_dell%27arte) "Gianduja (commedia dell'arte)"), a local [commedia dell'arte](/wiki/Commedia_dell%27arte "Commedia dell'arte") mask. Moreover, the city is also known for the so-called *[bicerin](/wiki/Bicerin "Bicerin")*, a traditional hot drink made of [espresso](/wiki/Espresso "Espresso"), [drinking chocolate](/wiki/Drinking_chocolate "Drinking chocolate") and [whole milk](/wiki/Whole_milk "Whole milk") served layered in a small rounded glass. Every year Turin organises *CioccolaTÒ*, a two-week chocolate festival run with the main Piedmontese chocolate producers, such as [Caffarel](/wiki/Pierre_Paul_Caffarel "Pierre Paul Caffarel"), Streglio, [Venchi](/wiki/Venchi "Venchi") and others, as well as some big international companies, such as [Lindt & Sprüngli](/wiki/Lindt_%26_Spr%C3%BCngli "Lindt & Sprüngli").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Aperitivo_al_bar.jpg/250px-Aperitivo_al_bar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aperitivo_al_bar.jpg)

A typical Italian *[aperitivo](/wiki/Italian_meal_structure#Formal_meal_structure "Italian meal structure")*

As for [snack food](/wiki/Snack_food "Snack food"), the now popular *[tramezzini](/wiki/Tramezzino "Tramezzino")* were first served in a historic café of downtown Turin, namely *Caffè Mulassano*, where they were devised in 1925 as an alternative to [English](/wiki/Cuisine_of_England "Cuisine of England") [tea sandwiches](/wiki/Tea_sandwich "Tea sandwich").[[83]](#cite_note-85)[[84]](#cite_note-86) In recent years, another trademark drink of the city is *MoleCola*, an Italian [cola](/wiki/Cola "Cola") that entered production in 2012 and quickly spread both in Italy and outside its native country.[[85]](#cite_note-87)

Local cuisine also features a particular type of pizza, so-called *[pizza al padellino](/wiki/Pizza#Italy "Pizza")* or *[pizza al tegamino](/wiki/Pizza#Italy "Pizza")*, which is basically a small-sized, thick-crust and deep-dish pizza typically served in several Turin pizza places.[[86]](#cite_note-88)[[87]](#cite_note-89)[[88]](#cite_note-90)

Since the mid-1980s, Piedmont has also benefited from the start of the [Slow Food](/wiki/Slow_Food "Slow Food") movement and [Terra Madre](/wiki/Terra_Madre "Terra Madre"), events that have highlighted the rich agricultural and vinicultural value of the [Po Valley](/wiki/Po_Valley "Po Valley") and northern Italy.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Palazzo_dell%27Universit%C3%A0_%28Turin%29.jpg/250px-Palazzo_dell%27Universit%C3%A0_%28Turin%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palazzo_dell%27Universit%C3%A0_(Turin).jpg)

Hall of the Rectorate Palace of the University of Turin

Turin is home to one of Italy's oldest universities, the [University of Turin](/wiki/University_of_Turin "University of Turin"), including its affiliated [Collegio Carlo Alberto](/wiki/Collegio_Carlo_Alberto "Collegio Carlo Alberto"), which ranks among the best universities in the country. Another established university in the city is the [Polytechnic University of Turin](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Turin "Polytechnic University of Turin"), ranking among Top 50 universities in the world and #1 in Italy in the fields of engineering, technology and computer science ("Academic Ranking of World Universities" published by [Shanghai Jiao Tong University](/wiki/Shanghai_Jiao_Tong_University "Shanghai Jiao Tong University")). Turin also hosts the [United Nations System Staff College](/wiki/United_Nations_System_Staff_College "United Nations System Staff College"), the [European Training Foundation](/wiki/European_Training_Foundation "European Training Foundation"), and a campus of the [ESCP](/wiki/ESCP_Europe "ESCP Europe") business school, ranked among the 10 best business schools in Europe. Moreover, the city hosts three small English language post-secondary institutions: [St. John International University](/wiki/St._John_International_University "St. John International University"), [International University College of Turin](/wiki/International_University_College_of_Turin "International University College of Turin"), and the [Turin School of Development](/wiki/Turin_School_of_Development "Turin School of Development").

In 2025, Turin becomes the first city in Italy with a plan to combat [Islamophobia](/wiki/Islamophobia "Islamophobia").[[89]](#cite_note-91)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Transport")]

See also: [Gruppo Torinese Trasporti](/wiki/Gruppo_Torinese_Trasporti "Gruppo Torinese Trasporti"), [ToBike](/wiki/ToBike "ToBike"), and [Turin Metro](/wiki/Turin_Metro "Turin Metro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Arriere-gare-torino-porta-susa.jpg/250px-Arriere-gare-torino-porta-susa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arriere-gare-torino-porta-susa.jpg)

[Porta Susa](/wiki/Porta_Susa "Porta Susa") railway station

The city currently has a large number of rail and road work sites. Although this activity has increased as a result of the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"), parts of it had long been planned. Some of the work sites deal with general roadworks to improve traffic flow, such as underpasses and flyovers, but two projects are of major importance and will radically change the shape of the city.

One is the *Spina Centrale* ('Central Spine') project which includes the doubling of a major railway crossing the city, the [Turin-Milan railway](/wiki/Turin-Milan_railway "Turin-Milan railway") locally known as *Passante Ferroviario di Torino* ('Turin Railway Bypass'). The railroad previously ran in a trench, which will now be covered by a major boulevard running from north to south of Turin, in a central position along the city. [Porta Susa](/wiki/Torino_Porta_Susa_railway_station "Torino Porta Susa railway station"), on this section, will become Turin's main station to substitute the terminus of [Porta Nuova](/wiki/Torino_Porta_Nuova_railway_station "Torino Porta Nuova railway station") with a through station. Other important stations are [Stura](/wiki/Torino_Stura_railway_station "Torino Stura railway station"), [Rebaudengo](/wiki/Torino_Rebaudengo_Fossata_railway_station "Torino Rebaudengo Fossata railway station"), [Lingotto](/wiki/Torino_Lingotto_railway_station "Torino Lingotto railway station") and [Madonna di Campagna](/wiki/Madonna_di_Campagna "Madonna di Campagna") railway stations, though not all of them belong to the layout of the *Spina Centrale*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Metro_Torino_station_Fermi.JPG/250px-Metro_Torino_station_Fermi.JPG)](/wiki/File:Metro_Torino_station_Fermi.JPG)

Turin [Fermi](/wiki/Fermi_(Turin_Metro) "Fermi (Turin Metro)") metro station

The other major project is the construction of a subway line based on the [VAL](/wiki/V%C3%A9hicule_Automatique_L%C3%A9ger "Véhicule Automatique Léger") system, known as [Metrotorino](/wiki/Turin_Metro "Turin Metro"). This project is expected to continue for years and to cover a larger part of the city, but its first phase was finished in time for the [2006 Olympic Games](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"), inaugurated on 4 February 2006 and opened to the public the day after. The first leg of the subway system linked the nearby town of [Collegno](/wiki/Collegno "Collegno") with Porta Susa in Turin's city centre. On 4 October 2007, the line was extended to Porta Nuova and then, in March 2011, to Lingotto. A new extension of the so-called *Linea 1* ('Line 1') is expected in the near future, reaching both [Rivoli](/wiki/Rivoli,_Piedmont "Rivoli, Piedmont") (up to Cascine Vica hamlet) in the Western belt of Turin and *Piazza Bengasi* in the southeast side of the city. Furthermore, a *Linea 2* is in the pipeline that will connect the south-western district of Mirafiori with Barriera di Milano in the north end. In June 2018, the project entered the public consultation phase with the proposed list of 23 stations published on the city's website.[[90]](#cite_note-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Airbus_A320_of_Lufthansa_with_sharklets_%28D-AIZS%29.jpg/250px-Airbus_A320_of_Lufthansa_with_sharklets_%28D-AIZS%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Airbus_A320_of_Lufthansa_with_sharklets_(D-AIZS).jpg)

[Turin Caselle International Airport](/wiki/Turin_Caselle_Airport "Turin Caselle Airport")

The main street in the city centre, *Via Roma*, runs atop a tunnel built during the fascist era (when *Via Roma* itself was totally refurbished and took on its present-day aspect). The tunnel was supposed to host the underground line, but it is now used as an underground car park. A project to build an underground system was ready in the 1970s, with government funding for it and for similar projects in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") and Rome. Whilst the other two cities went ahead with the projects, Turin's local government led by mayor Diego Novelli shelved the proposal as it believed it to be too costly and unnecessary.

The city has an international airport known as [Caselle International Airport](/wiki/Turin_Caselle_Airport "Turin Caselle Airport") [Sandro Pertini](/wiki/Sandro_Pertini "Sandro Pertini") (airport code: TRN), in [Caselle Torinese](/wiki/Caselle_Torinese "Caselle Torinese"), about 13 km (8 mi) from Turin's centre—connected to the city by rail (from Dora Station) and bus (from Porta Nuova and Porta Susa railway stations).

From 2010 to 2023 a [bicycle sharing system](/wiki/Bicycle_sharing_system "Bicycle sharing system"), the [ToBike](/wiki/ToBike "ToBike"), was operational.

The metropolitan area is served by [Turin metropolitan railway service](/wiki/Turin_metropolitan_railway_service "Turin metropolitan railway service").

Central districts are served by [tram](/wiki/Trams_in_Turin "Trams in Turin"); lines 3, 4, and 9 are [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Tram_in_Torino%2C_Italy.jpg/250px-Tram_in_Torino%2C_Italy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tram_in_Torino,_Italy.jpg)

A city tram. A bus can be seen behind.

### Public transportation statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Turin&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Public transportation statistics")]

The average amount of time people spend commuting with public transit in Turin (for example, to and from work) on a weekday is 65 minutes. 14% of public transit riders ride for more than 2 hours every day. The average time spent waiting at a stop or station for public transit is 14 minutes, while 19% of riders wait for over 20 minutes on average every day. The average distance people usually ride in a single trip with public transit is 5.9 km (3.7 mi), while 9% travel more than 12 km (7.5 mi) in a single direction.[[91]](#cite_note-93)
